Since then, believers (Catholics) have been flocking Kibeho to witness Virgin Mary's Apparitions and to pay homage.
The Apparitions were confirmed and recognised by the Catholic Church in 2001. Since then, the pilgrimages to the shrine take place twice every year, in August and December.
Despite thousands of local people that trek to Kibeho and those that come from regional countries and overseas, the opportunities presented by the holy shrine remain largely untapped.
Its potential as a religious tourist attraction has not been exploited by the church or the government to boost tourism arrivals and earnings.
As I was going to the shrine, I noticed that the road from Huye District to Kibeho is relatively good.
However, commercial activities at the shrine present a contrasting picture; they are dominated by petty trade, and vendors of religious symbols, like the rosary, crucifixes, and portraits of the Virgin Mary and Jesus Christ, among others. The vendors operate in somewhat disorganised manner. Foodstuffs, like 'local' cakes, French fries, and cassava, are sold in unhygienic and dusty stalls. A keen observer will soon realise that there is a lot that has to be done to improve the situation in the two cases above.
Religious items are on high demand on this day; therefore a trade bazaar in these items is ideal and would present the vendors a competitive advantage pricewise.
Ngaruko Slyviesten, a vendor I found selling such items on a dirty carpet spread out on the ground, shared his experience.
"Because we are operating in an unhygienic environment, buyers take advantage to pay little," he says.
One would ask about the opportunities presented by the shrine to locals and other 'serious' investors.
Hospitality and catering
Notre Damme Motel is the only modern facility that provides accommodation and descent food to pilgrims. It is strategically located at the entrance of the shrine.
When I visited the facility on Assumption Day last year, I found that all rooms had been booked the previous day, mostly by international and regional pilgrims. In fact, some pilgrims spend nights in open air sort of camps or makeshift shelters.
The demand for food was overwhelming, especially after Assumption Day service (marked on August 15 each year). The facility had underestimated the demand. I later learnt that they had just opened shop at the shrine, which could partly explain the challenge.
I realised that an enterprising business person can reap big at the shrine and in Kibeho neighbourhood if they did their homework well.
For instance, the food served by vendors is the kind that will 'kill' your appetite. Local porridge (igikoma) sold in dirty jerrycans by women from the neighbourhood as common item on the menu.
In fact, there is only one stall selling 'real' food. A middle-aged man, his wife and daughter are behind the food stall. At first, he ignores me. When he finally agrees to talk to me, Diojene Uwanyiriginje, says the job (food vending) helps him to supplement his meager income as a driver.
Unlike most of the stalls manned by porridge vendors, his is fairly clean and organised.
The food is, however, cold and looks like it was prepared overnight. To make matters worse, he has no heating facilities. With the number of clients growing by the minute, he is forced to serve the next clients on one bigger dish. They cannot complain because there is nowhere else to buy food.
His next clients are regional pilgrims as they can barely speak Kinyarwanda; they look skeptical and reluctant, but have no option but to eat the food.
Later, Uwanyiringije tells me it good the vendors are not taxed. On modernising and improving the workplace, he argues that that comes with additional operational costs. He however agrees that the place designated for selling different items at the shrine needs a face-lift.
Transport
Many pilgrims who flock the shrine every year use public means. The road from Huye to the holy shrine in Kibeho is relatively good, but not tarmac.
When one is going to the shrine, they face no challenge. However, going back after mass in the afternoon is another story. There are a multitude of pilgrims, but a handful of public transport vehicles. We waited for over two hours before we could get a taxi to Huye town.
This is a clear indication that transporters are 'sleeping on the job'. This should be seen as an opportunity public transporters working or living in Huye District to make an extra buck. Besides, this would be killing two birds with one stone; providing a solution and making money.
Cultural tourism and free marketing for Rwanda
Rwanda is a beautiful country that has a lot to offer to regional and international people visiting.
Rwanda's unique handicrafts and traditional dances can be promoted on the international and regional scene during this period. We could take advantage of the presence of the international media during these events to market the country as a top tourist destination.
So, entertainment and trade fairs, showcasing handicrafts and our unique culture can be organised prior and after the Assumption Day celebrations and other activities in a seamless manner.
Padre Ildephonse Bizimungu, a senior priest at Kibeho Holy Shrine, believes continued collaboration between the government and Catholic Church will could help promote Kibeho as a strong religious tourism destination.
Fr Bizimungu appreciates government's support to the Holy Shrine and the community, including provision of security and healthcare services, especially when there are thousands of pilgrims at the shrine in August and December.
"A lot more can be done to promote this place as a religious tourism destination, and a vibrant business centre.
Government should work on things like roads, marketing and also give incentives to local entrepreneurs to come and establish businesses at the shrine," says Bizimungu.
"The church is receptive to good ideas as long as they won't hinder the purpose of pilgrimage."

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices Stable in Eastern Province
Headline: Rwanda: Food Prices Stable in Eastern Province
Body_Text:
Commodity and foodstuffs prices are largely stable in most markets in the Eastern Province. The price of Irish potatoes has gone down in Nyagatare District, with a kilo going for Rwf220 in Nyagatare and Kabarore markets, down from Rwf230 the previous week.
Sweet potatoes cost Rwf190 per kilogramme, cassava tubers are at Rwf180 per kilo, onions cost Rwf800 a kilo and beef is at Rwf1,800 per kilo in Kayonza Market.
A kilogramme of maize flour goes for Rwf500, rice costs between Rwf800 a kilogramme, and that of salt is at Rwf400.
A kilo of fresh peas increased to Rwf1,200in Nyagatare Market, from Rwf1,00, while fresh beans cost Rwf450 a kilo, up from Rwf400.
A basinful of tomatoes costs Rwf4,500 unchanged from last week, bag of charcoal ranges from Rwf6,500 to Rwf7,000 in Kiramuruzi market.
Passion fruits go for Rwf1,200 a kilo in Kayonza market, mangoes are at Rwf800 per kilogramme, and a kilo of cassava flour is at Rwf350, sorghum flour cost Rwf800, millet flour cost Rwf1, 000 in Rwamagana market.
Banana fingers cost Rwf200 a kilo, while yams are at Rwf270 per kilogramme in Rwamagana market.
Pumpkins range from Rwf400 to Rwf600 each, ground nuts cost Rwf1,000, paw paws are at between Rwf400 and Rwf500, depending on size, while cabbage ranges from Rwf400-Rwf300 each in Ntunga Market.

Original_Search_Title: 'My Writing Captures the Essence of Me and My People'
Headline: Nigeria: 'My Writing Captures the Essence of Me and My People'
Body_Text:
Su'eddie Vershima Agema is the author of three books, two poetry books and a collection of short stories. He is a joint winner of the ANA Poetry Award and the founder of SEHVAGE Publishers as well as Chairman of the Association of Nigeria Authors, Benue State Chapter. He talks here about his books, his passion for literature and foray into publishing.
How was Su'eddie, the writer, not the individual, born? What were the things that shaped your decision to become a writer?
My big brothers, Taver, Gabriel and Sever used to draw comics and write intriguing stories which they never completed despite my pleas. I remember wanting to always finish my brothers' tales! My sister, Theodora, was always the poet. My father was a story teller and my mother bought me lots of books of the African Writers Series when I was little. I learnt to read late but eventually, when I started I had some foundation to build on. Creativity has thus been there as a foundation for me. I started drawing comics right up to secondary school but somewhere, the scribbles started. I never took writing serious and concentrated more on comics and the likes. I think I also wrote a few uncompleted short stories in secondary school but nothing serious. However, by the time I got to the university, I had started writing scattered lines of what I felt were poetry. I shudder when I see some of those things today. Now, I was going through my secondary school slum book last year or so and was shocked to see that for future ambition, I wrote 'Lecturer and Renowned Writer.' So, maybe the decision was always there. And yes, life shaped it.
How intriguing. As a writer of prose and poetry, what determined the genre in which you express any idea?
The piece many times determines itself. I write with what inspiration comes to my head and discover after a while that what I thought was meant to be a story breaks into lines and stanzas, flowing in verse that leaves me wondering. Other times, I start a poem then find it stretching itself. That said, some poems have created fiction for me and out of some fiction I have written, poetry has been born. There are times when the same idea or experience creates both. The key thing is that no matter how it ends, whether as poetry or prose, I always find a way to tweak it while playing with the language so that there's some finesse to it. I love beautiful language use and always try to adapt it to my work.
Your poetry touches on many things, in Home Equals Holes, for instance, there is a lot of reminiscence, love, loss and recollections. What influenced the concerns that were captured in this collection?
I believe that poetry is an expression of our innermost feelings in beautiful language that takes people to another level. Naturally then, the poems in my collections are largely derived from the various experiences that we go through, as exiles on earth. That's the thread that inspired the final cloth that became it.
Why did you feel it necessary to include smatterings of Tiv in some of the poems in this collection?
There's a double answer to that. In some cases, I did not consciously set out to put 'smatterings' of Tiv while in others, there was that attempt. For the first part, I am Tiv and I think in Tiv a lot so it is only natural that sometimes, parts of my work would come out thus. On the other note, I can say, some of my writings capture the full essence of myself and my people. There is the hope that this wouldn't be lost so I capture it in what small ways to preserve it while showcasing its beauty which cannot be felt in any other language than Tiv.
How important do you think identity is to a writer? And when did you become conscious of wanting to project your identity through your writings?
I think identity is very important as we all have unique stories to tell from our unique positions. It helps to be able to build a voice that is distinct whether telling our stories or telling the stories of others. We have to own what we have and truly show who we are in ways that will portray our essence. Now, there's nothing wrong in stepping out of one's shoe to be different sometimes but the key thing is to truly be you without being an ape of someone else. I do not consciously set out to project my identity in writing, to be honest. I guess what might stand out in that light is largely something subconscious because if you know who you are, you do not really need to think about it when being you, whether in writing or living.
When you write, how self-conscious are you as a writer? I ask because you had indicated that with your second collection "Home Equals Holes" you felt the need to check yourself whereas with your first, "Bring Our Casket Home" you were unrestrained. Do you think this is a problem that plagues writers after publishing several books?
I am quite self-conscious in my writings. I have come to realise the power of words and what they can do. Once, I could easily write and let things go, then sometimes I would find fortune favour my scribbles in one way or the other. These days, almost every word has a reason and I usually have to ask myself if the message I am portraying is truly what I want to project. I am learning to let go because sometimes being too self-conscious inhibits creativity. I am also learning to be open to new styles and directions so in a few cases, I close the eyes of my heart and just flow my soul away without restriction, smiling at whatever comes out.
Your short story collection, The Bottom of Another Tale, has a strong strand of faith and the supernatural in it. Was this something you aimed for deliberately?
In many cases, I would say yes but not in all. I believe that faith plays a great part in our existence and there's more to the supernatural than meets the eyes. It helps us if we get this into perspective. With that in view, you can then decide to ignore it or hold on to it. The key thing in the end is that whether by stroke of fate, strength of faith or the power of the supernatural, whatever happens to us ultimately is determined by our actions. That is the spirit of most of the book.
Some of your characters, even the most skeptical ones, eventually end up being prevailed upon by tradition, culture or superstition. What were you trying to convey with this?
That's debatable! I can argue in the opposite but let's not waste too much energy there. I believe strongly in tradition and culture. I think also that the word 'superstition' is largely suspect because what someone calls faith and religion in one context might be superstition in the other. What I was trying to convey is that we ought to respect our cultures and traditions. It doesn't matter whether you believe in it or not. The key thing is not to ignore them or look down on them simply because we are now more Western or more religious as Christians, Muslims or yes, traditionalists. So, respect every tradition and culture but believe in what you will, and generally hope for the best.
Twenty-six stories in a collection. Why so many? What informed the decision and selections of stories that eventually featured in this collection?
I don't think the stories are too many considering they are of varying length. The stories in the collection were chosen by myself and my editors at our publishing house. We tried to connect themes and the spirit of what we hoped to achieve. The key thing was to write an entertaining book that would help educate and excite without being redundant. We also tried to take stories that would cover as many aspects as possible; love, romance, adventure, politics and everything else so that there would be something for everyone in such a way as would leave glad.
Nigerian writer, EC Osondu recently said that the short story is not a try-out for a novel. Is this an idea you share or are your short stories a try-out for a novel perhaps?
Eh, I don't completely agree. Some short stories are try outs for novels. You can't help but get the feel of that when you read such tales. There are writers, like Abubakar Adam Ibrahim - easily using 'The Whispering Trees' as an example, Pever X, Aondosoo Labe, Agatha Aduro, Debbie Iorliam, to mention a minute few from my circles, who do this near effortlessly. It is like, you pick a short story and when you get to its end, you have the feeling of a novel. In such situations, what do we say?
Your works have been shortlisted for the ANA Prizes and you have won a couple of prizes. How have these nominations and prizes impacted on your work?
Prizes generally give one a good feeling. It is always nice to have a nomination here or a prize there. They make you know that you are doing something right, at least. I have learnt though not to rely on prizes or affirmations from establishments to know my worth. I will be honest though and say that the prizes have also made me sit up knowing that I have a responsibility. Each prize puts a responsibility on my shoulder letting me know that I have to work harder to fulfill the trust of whatever honour it was I got. There's also that aspect of ensuring that other writers who look up to such prizes would be inspired to know that a prize is not really the end thing but the starting point where you have to put in more so that you remain relevant in hearts - which is the true prize of every writer.
You are not only a writer but a publisher as well. At what point did you realise you needed to create SEVHAGE and what is the idea behind the publishing outfit?
I can't say exactly when the realization came. I think it is an idea that kept playing against events till it became concrete with registration in 2012. SEVHAGE was borne out of that desire to give writers a better deal than most of what we have around. Most of my friends kept complaining about one issue or the other and I had my share of complaints too. There were issues of printers who simply print your work and leave it an orphan. Others who sell your work without your knowledge while they keep the money. There were also issues of low quality in terms of output and content. Summary is at some point, the frustration of all this gets to one especially when you keep hearing one more complaint or the other, then feel the frustration from those who you hold dear. It is worse when you see works that could have been done better produced distastefully. What was the option? While there are a few standard publishing houses around, it was obvious that they weren't enough, particularly in our parts here. The first idea was to collaborate with one or the other of the publishing houses. I sent my ideas to them and put across proposals but to no avail. In the end, we had to move into the field and do our best growing at our pace to give writers a better deal traditionally where possible and where not, getting some funds from them to help facilitate their dreams. It is a passion that has held us on.
As a publisher working in Nigeria, what would you say have been the greatest challenges?
We have the problem of distribution which reduces the spread of our works. There is also the rising price of paper which makes the publishing process far more expensive with each passing day. Another problem is that of pirates. It is bad enough that not every book gets the deserved attention from the reading public despite several efforts. When it does get that attention, if you are not careful, the pirates cash in on it before you can make a buck. Imagine us working on books of a writer like Servio Gbadamosi, T. J Benson, or Sibbyl Whyte, growing them till they get big. Then at the last minute when the book becomes a hit, the pirates come! You lose your investments and in many cases, your spirit. Then, yes, there doesn't seem to be any policy support from government to help the industry. So, in the end, publishers are really on their own, left to their fate to fight a battle where many times they sacrifice their soul for passions they hold dear.
Considering the clime and the lack of policy support from the government for the publishing industry, do you think it is a viable business to be in?
Well, it depends on what type of publishing one is into. As a firm that is primarily literary, I think the honest answer is no, not really. The risks and the challenges plus the realities do not really make publishing viable. Now, that is talking of publishing strictly as a business. You discover most publishers often need to have a side job or something for sustenance.
Despite the challenges still, you have publishing houses like Parresia and Cassava Republic and Farafina repositioning and you have new ones like AMAB books joining the fray. Where is the optimism coming from?
I will talk about us as a group. The optimism largely comes from love and passion. I daresay that Nigerian publishers are about the most passionate people you will find around here. Most publishers came into the business based on passion and love for books and writers. We have great faith in what we do. We look beyond bleak skies knowing that the skies will bring rains to water the earth and after that, sunshine will come to make everything warm and beautiful. How fast and how well? Time will tell. So, we look on in hope as other lovers join the fray, ignorantly or with open eyes, putting hands together in belief that the day will come when all will be well for writers, readers and us all. After all, in the end, true love wins, no?
Interestingly, a Ghanaian blogger mentioned that the only Nigerian product she could readily find in Ghana are books. How do statements like this make you feel?
Glad that our efforts aren't totally in vain. There's nothing as beautiful for a publisher as getting your work out there and having it read. So, the wider the spread and the availability, the more warming it is.
You hosted a festival in Makurdi recently to celebrate the fourth anniversary of SEVHAGE. Why a festival? What is the idea behind that?
The idea of the festival was to start something here that is more popular in other parts of the country. Before we started though, we did a survey to find out what literary enthusiasts around here would want us to do. The general response included a festival and you can imagine, giving loads of book gifts in addition to increasing the visibility of writers and books. We took up the challenge and yes, went to work. It was challenging considering there wasn't much time to put it together or the usual, funds. However, we did it and with Aondo's favour, it was a success.
Is this a one off festival or is it something you hope will continue in the next few years?
Definitely not a one-off thing. We hope to continue the SEVHAGE Literary Festival making it better as time goes on in the coming years. Plans are already underway to ensure the next edition is far better than the last. Fingers crossed on that and a prayer.
As the Chairman of ANA Benue State, how relevant do you think associations like ANA have been to the development of writers in the country?
I daresay that more than half of the growth that has come to be in the Nigerian literary sector has come through the efforts of literary associations. This is especially as there is little or no support from government or big organisations. In the end, writers and passionate literary enthusiasts come together to create platforms that will help elevate the writer and the literary sector. These associations always come up with programmes that help to energise writers, promote them and create a platform where they can interact with themselves and also with readers. I am a product of associations like this. You meet a lot of people who you ordinarily wouldn't have met. See the conventions! You meet your heroes and so many other interesting people. Through critique sessions you also get to sharpen your work. Add that to the literary competitions they offer in addition to all the other programmes like workshops that they put and you would understand. Do you want to talk of the guest writer sessions and readings? I belong to a number of associations including Abuja Writers' Forum, Abuja Literary Society, Writers' League of the Benue State University, Purple Silver, and under SEVHAGE, we have our literary meetings too. Each time, I go for any meeting, I come back refreshed. It is true that every association has its challenges but without them all in whatever capacity or at whatever level, the Nigerian literary sector would perish. Quote me on that anywhere.
What would be your response to claims that ANA has not lived up to expectations of writers? How would you suggest the association be repositioned?
Now, to be honest most of these expectations have not been met but the question is how much have the writers contributed towards building ANA and positioning it in such a way that it can meet their expectations? We have a lot of unregistered members and members who don't even pay their dues. Not too many people are committed to contributing ideas or supporting initiatives of the association. Running an association where you have to source funds, formulate ideas and generally just push activities forward isn't easy. That said, how can we reposition the association? All writers need to take ANA as something that is personal. Contribute your time and finances to the association. The CVs of a lot of people carry the membership of the association but how many of them are really registered or contribute to it? You have people who only come for the national convention once a year and ignore the state branches. So, let's start with us-members. Let's pay our dues in cash, in intellect and in every capacity possible. We can work with our local branches to make it better. If you think there aren't enough prizes, why don't you create one or bring suggestions? If you think the conventions or even the association is getting too political, why don't you suggest ways to make them better or contribute to make it less political? Heck, you can even contest, win and bring in the changes you want! We have prominent writers and members who get busier and watch the association from afar. From wherever they are, they simply say things about the association and its flaws. Tell them to contribute o! We can't leave it all to Denja Abdullahi, the President at the top and/or the local Executives. More than all of these, the national executive should work hand in hand with the state Executive Committees across the federation to create programmes that will favour writers at every level. In the end, it is easy to complain but as Eleanor Roosevelt said, it is better to light a candle than curse the darkness. We all have to do our bit to meet all our expectations.

Original_Search_Title: Ankara Press Is Going Against Romance Stereotypes - Shercliff
Headline: Nigeria: Ankara Press Is Going Against Romance Stereotypes - Shercliff
Body_Text:
Emma Shercliff is a director of Cassava Republic Press (UK) and the Publishing Director of Ankara Press. She is a PhD candidate at the Institute of Education, University of London, where her doctoral research explores and documents the role of female publishers in shaping the literary landscape in Africa. Presently based in Abuja, she spoke to Bookshelf about the mission Ankara Press, how African publishing should be viewed, roles of literary networks and much more.
What were you doing before you delved into the publishing business?
I went into publishing straight from university, so I didn't really have a professional life before then. When I was at the university I had experience with different publishing companies within the UK. I went to Harper Collins Publishers, Orion Publishing Group and the Children's publisher, Orchard Books. So I got a feel of what publishing was like and then I applied for a graduate scheme. So I have been publishing for a long time, since 1997.
Do you edit?
Yes, but now I do a mixture. My background was initially sales and marketing and then editorial before becoming managing director.
You're a director of Cassava Republic Press and the publishing director of Ankara Press. How did this happen?
I came to Abuja two years ago and doing a PhD looking at publishing in Africa, specifically at women in the publishing industry. I wanted some field research, to spend some time with a publisher and here in Abuja the obvious person to call and ask to spend time with was Bibi Bakare-Yusuf, founder of Cassava Republic. She's a phenomenal publisher. That's how it started. She was so generous and said, yes, come. So I spend time there and do a little bit of consultancy on the business and gradually became more and more involved, specifically in the project I was working on - the launch of Ankara Press in December 2014. So that's how I became involved. Now I am going with Bibi in the UK where we will set up a branch of Cassava Republic in London. So, my involvement is very much with Bibi and the UK business, even though I am right here in Abuja.
Why a PhD specifically on women in the African publishing industry?
It came really after the research I did for my Master's where I was looking at the lack of female writers. That was when I became interested in whether there's a relationship with the lack of female publishers at that time in the 1960s or 70s. With what you see now, particularly in the UK, there are a lot of women in the publishing industry, but very few own their own companies. So actually, it still remains the case that a lot of the older and more established educational publishers, those in Ibadan for example, very few of them have women on their board. I want to look at the relationship between that and, if what comes out in the end is due to the lack of women in the industry.
From your research so far, what have you discovered?
From my initial and very preliminary research, with the interviews I have done with some of the managing directors of the big companies, I think basically once women get to a certain level; it's difficult for them to progress above it. But there are very few women who work in sales and marketing because it's a physical job here in Nigeria. There's a lot of moving of books around and driving, which is a job too difficult for women to do. So, even when women applied and were taken for those roles they often did not last long in those jobs. It's not because they don't want to do the job, but that the environment is not conducive.
Okay, back to Ankara Press. Was there any reason it launched with six books in 2014?
We wanted to publish commercial fiction. Also, when people bought one or two books and were excited by them, we wanted to have enough books for them to continue reading. We hope that we will eventually publish six books each year. We are now doing two and will release four more before the end of this year. We have had a lot of manuscripts coming in and reading through them is certainly taking me longer than I have anticipated. The idea was to release another six this Valentine's Day, but trying to publish six simultaneously is a lot of work. For the designer, it's also far easier and quicker to work on two titles at a time and much more streamlined.
What was the experience like publishing the six books?
It was absolutely Bibi's idea to have a romance imprint. And there are a couple of reasons for it. One of them is, for any publisher to become sustainable, there's the need to be commercially successful. And this means fiction for the young; crime and romance - essentially those areas of publishing which open up large markets. Literary fiction is wonderful and sometimes you get a big hit but those books don't always sell in huge quantities. The second reason, about which Bibi was very clear, was that she wanted to provide what she called 'empowering romance.' So she said, I looked at the romance we have in Nigeria and it doesn't cut across a large part of the country, most of it is imported, such as Harlequin. It's very widely read, but it doesn't feature African characters. So she wanted to provide African romance and also show relationships that were balanced and fulfilling and not the stereotypes of aggressive male and submissive female. And the other thing was it also had to be entertaining.
How easy or difficult was it to select the first six manuscripts?
The first six were already commissioned before I came to Ankara Press. That process took a long time. But I can tell you about the later submissions where we had quite a lot and some of them were quite frankly, not very good. We work hard on the editorial side. One of the two books we are publishing this Valentine's Day, 'The Seeing Place,' is about an upcoming actor, where the casting director was originally a man and the upcoming actress was a woman and we thought that went with the stereotype, so we reversed that and now we have the casting director as the woman and the upcoming actor as a man.
Do you think romance novels are the way to go in today's publishing?
To me it's a part of the business I am really excited about. Because when people see this new series they get really excited, pick them up and say, yes I want to read these. We have had really good response and I think partly it's because there is nothing like it in the market. We have got coverage on CNN, CNBC, and the Guardian UK. People are just genuinely interested.
Do you have any writing background?
In Nigeria many of the publishers are writers. It's very different from other countries where I have worked so far - the UK, Australia and the United States. If you want to be a writer be a writer, if you want to be a publisher become a publisher. So I never wanted to be a writer but a publisher.
Why do you think the reverse is the case in Nigeria?
I don't know. I am thinking more about the contemporary publishers, about Parresia or Farafina where some of the staff are also writers. There are advantages to being a writer because you are in a writing community. But for me, there's that clear distinction between the two and for me it's publishing. Publishing should be seen as a career in itself.
Some publishers in Nigeria probably evolved due to the lack of publishers to publish their works and so they decided to alter the status quo. What would you say about that?  Shercliff: I think the establishment of literary networks like Farafina, Kwani and Chimurenga came from that background. One thing for me that's remarkable about the African publishing industry is the mutual support, whatever country you come from. We have had a lot of help from Kwani and Chimurenga and vice versa. There are just few of us so we work to support each other and it's mutually beneficial.
Where does your passion for the job come from?
I love books and reading. I really enjoy the business side; the sales, marketing and publicity. For a lot of people when they think about a publisher, they think if you are publishing it's just the editorial. But an important part of it is getting the marketing right, getting the product across the market at the right price. For me that's where I feel I can make a difference.
What challenges do you think the publishing business is facing in Nigeria?
I have been to many countries in publishing, probably about 40. Here in Nigeria, number one is the lack of a good editorial staff. I think it's very difficult to find good editors here and that's partly because of the lack of training. There's the need to have training schemes. If it's seen as a profession, people might go into it like how they go into banking or become a lawyer. I think that the sales distribution network is tricky and the retail sector is not developed enough, even though the NPA and the Booksellers Association are doing some good work, there's a long way to go. If you publish a book, trying to get it out into the market is a bit costly and another issue is that the printing here in Nigeria is not very good. The difference between what's printed in India and Nigeria is very clear.
Any particular reason for releasing your books on Valentine's Day?
Its very nice timing for us and we decided to hang onto the two new books just published until then. It's a time of love and romance.
Can you give readers some insight into the two latest novels, 'Love Next Door' and 'The Seeing Place'?
'The Seeing Place' to be released tomorrow, February 14th, is by a new Ankara Press author - Aziza Eden Walker. It's set in Cape Town, this is the one that has the upcoming male actor and female casting director. So she casts him and they form a bond and eventually everything culminates. Then the other, 'Love Next Door,' is written by Amina Thula, whom we have already published in the first wave of books. It's set in Johannesburg and is about a girl who moves into an apartment and luckily enough, meets this next door neighbour who is a teacher as well as a sculptor.
What does Ankara Press hope to achieve with romance fiction?
Our mission is to publish a new kind of romance. We want to provide readers in Africa with stories set in Africa with African characters, storylines and setting, so that they wouldn't always have to read about love in the West. But I think also, one of the things we are really keen to do, is provide examples of relationships that are fulfilling and loving, fun and entertaining. There's sex in some of them, but not all of them. No relationship is a stereotype or conforms to a particular formula and what we are trying to do with these novels is give examples of lots of different types of relationships and show that even with slight problems they could be fulfilling in many different ways. So, yes, it's about providing entertainment that Africans can relate to.

Original_Search_Title: Econet in Another Diaspora Remittances Partnership
Headline: Zimbabwe: Econet in Another Diaspora Remittances Partnership
Body_Text:
Econet Wireless through its Ecocash mobile finances services, has launched a new partnership for Europe remittances, known as Chitoro.
Chitoro offers money transfer methods that allow for both business to business (B2B) transfers and worldwide person to person (P2P) payments. The fees for remittances or transfers will range from 1,99 British Pounds. Recipients can choose to use the funds at any EcoCash point of sale service facility or to cash out the money at any EcoCash agent. They can also be paid out in cash at any Steward Bank or CABS branch
Chitoro, a subsidiary of Econet Wireless Group through Cassava Connent, is authorized by the Financial Conduct Authority in the UK to provide financial services across the UK and Europe as a prudent and world class remittance business.
Speaking at the launch of the partnership, Central Bank Deputy Governor Kupukile Mlambo said Diaspora remittances were a great source of Zimbabwe's liquidity.
"The Bank and policymakers are working hard to have policies and systems that encourage electronic money transfer, electronic banking and the use of plastic money.
Econet COO Fayaz King said Ecocash had come onto the market to change the lives of Zimbabweans for the better and since September 2011, the brand had innovated to bring in partnerships with other brands like Mastercard, Moneygram and Western Union.
"Today we witness another partnership, another growth, another driver to bring remittances into the country, to bring money to those who need it the most," said King.
King added that Ecocash had provided the conduit in which remittances can find their way into the country.
Ecocash General Manager Natalie Jabangwe said Econet did not want to remain only in providing telecommunications services and mobile financial services for the people of Zimbabwe but was determined to go to those uncharted places where no other telecommunication company in Zimbabwe has been.
"Econet is pleased to announce the partnership with Chitoro for international remittances. It gives us the ability to send money from the whole of the United Kingdom and many other countries that will follow," she said.
She added that the Chitoro service would available 24 hours a day, 365 a year, at a low cost and more convenient than the traditional remittance options available.
"It is secure, provides the best practice in terms of security and fraud management procedures and the steps to use it are easy, quick and convenient."
The money would be transferred to the recipient within seconds and the charges would depend on the amount of money to be sent, with no additional charges on the recipient to receive the money into their Ecocash wallet.
The normal Ecocash charges would however apply on Ecocash transactions like cash out, paying merchants and the ability to pay bills.
Chitoro's Director, Haxton Rose said the partnership with Ecocash has allowed Chitoro to operate on a much larger scale, giving them access to over 25 000 Ecocash agents and the ability to pay to over 5 million Ecocash wallets.
"For our customers, this is great news as it offers them more choice and convenience in sending money to Zimbabwe for their loved ones," said Rose.
"We understand the importance of providing value for our services that is why we will continue to ensure that with Chitoro our fees will always be competitive."

Original_Search_Title: Econet Merges With UK-Based Bureau De Change
Headline: Zimbabwe: Econet Merges With UK-Based Bureau De Change
Body_Text:
Telecoms operator Econet Zimbabwe has integrated with UK-based Bureau de Change, Chitoro, to offer receipt of transfers into Zimbabwe via the EcoCash platform.
Chitoro is an international money transfer business that allows customers to send money from a bank account, VISA or MasterCard and cash through its system.
The partnership will allow people in the United Kingdom among other countries across Europe to send money directly to a recipient's EcoCash wallet in either Zimbabwe or South Africa.
Chitoro is part of the Econet Wireless Group following its acquisition by Mauritian registered subsidiary, Cassava Connect. It is licensed by the Financial Conduct Authority in the UK to provide financial services across the UK and Europe as a prudent and world class remittance business.
Under the system, money would be transferred to the recipient within seconds and the charges would depend on the amount of money to be sent, with no additional charges on the recipient to receive the money into their EcoCash wallet.
The normal EcoCash charges would, however, apply on EcoCash transactions like cash out, paying merchants and the ability to pay bills.
Speaking at the launch yesterday, Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe Deputy Governor Dr Kuphukile Mlambo said the partnership is expected to increase remittances into the economy.
"We are glad, as the central bank, to see these two organisations coming together to provide a service that will provide relief to our customers and benefit our economy greatly. One of the key assets that we have in this country is a large Diaspora," said Dr Mlambo.
"As a dollarised economy we have very few channels of liquidity, it's either through your exports, through lines of credit and through Diaspora. Over time, Diaspora has become important because we cannot print money and we need to get it from somewhere."
Dr Mlambo said the collaboration supports the national objective of promoting systems that increase remittances to the economy through formal channels.
EcoCash general manager Mrs Natalie Jabangwe said that the relationship between EcoCash and Chitoro was completely commercial like any other agreements that were made with other international money transfer agents in the past.
"It is the same sort of straight to wallet transfer model that EcoCash has been facilitating with several other remittance outfits that include World Remit, Western Union, Mukuru and MoneyGram.
"Even though Chitoro is part of the Econet group, the relationship is purely commercial. As EcoCash we want to be the glue that connects the Diaspora and home," said Mrs Jabangwe.
She said by the end of August 2015, according to the financial results, 5 million people were signed up to the EcoCash platform.
Chitoro director Mr Haxton Rose said the partnership with Econet Wireless opens new and exciting opportunities.

Original_Search_Title: Foodstuff Prices Stable in Kigali City Markets
Headline: Rwanda: Foodstuff Prices Stable in Kigali City Markets
Body_Text:
Prices for most commodities are stable in most markets in Kigali city and suburbs. Irish potatoes go for Rwf190 a kilogramme, fresh peas cost Rwf1,200 per kilogramme, rice is at between Rwf700 and Rwf1,000 in most city markets, while beef cost Rwf2,300 per kilo.
Bananas cost Rwf210 per kilo in Kimironko market and are at Rwf200 in Nyabugogo market. A bar of soap is at Rwf500, salt costs Rwf200 per kilogramme, millet flour goes for Rwf1,220 a kilo, while that of maize flour costs Rwf600 per kilo in Nyarugenge and Kimironko markets. A kilo of sugar goes for Rwf800, bread (one kilogramme) is at between Rwf1,000 and Rwf1,200 in most city supermarkets, depending on the brand.
A kilogramme of beans is at Rwf500 in Remera market, and goes for Rwf550 in Kimironko market, while that of tomatoes costs Rwf500 and groundnuts are at Rwf1,250 per kilogramme in Kimironko market. A kilo of tomato tree (ibinyomoro) is at Rwf1, 300, in Nyarugenge market and cost Rwf1,200 in Nyabugogo, while pineapples (medium-sized) cost between Rwf400 and Rwf500 in Gakinjiro market.
Oranges now cost Rwf900 per kilo, and a cluster of yellow bananas is at Rwf800. The price of passion fruits is at Rwf1, 300 per kilo in most city markets, a pawpaw costs Rwf400; watermelons are at between Rwf2,000 and Rwf2,500 each.
Sweet potatoes cost Rwf250 per kilogramme; cassava is at Rwf200 per kilo, a five-litre jerry can of cooking oil goes for Rwf6,200. The price of onions went down by Rwf100 to Rwf700 per kilo, from Rwf800 last week.
Traders said the trend could continue in the coming weeks.

Original_Search_Title: Residents Invited for Gold Mining
Headline: Tanzania: Residents Invited for Gold Mining
Body_Text:
Dodoma — The government has expressed its readiness to engage communities surrounding Biharamulo goldmine in Kagera Region to enable the residents benefit from operations of the goldmine, Deputy Minister for Energy and Minerals, Medard Kalemani pledged in the parliament yesterday.
Formerly Tulawaka Goldmine operated by conglomerate Acacia Mining then known as Barrick Gold Africa, the investment is now owned and operated by STAMIGOLD, a subsidiary of the State Mining Corporation (STAMICO).
The Deputy Minister was prompted to make the explanation in response to a basic question by Biharamulo West MP, Mr Oscar Mukasa, who had complained that residents of Mavota village around the mine were not benefiting from its operations.
Mr Kalemani said the government was ready to provide the villagers with areas to conduct artisanal mining and training on how to acquire capital to improve their operations.
"The small miners will be encouraged to form groups to enable them benefit from subsidies provided by the government and the mine will provide them with training on how to conduct proper mining operations at small-scale level," he stated.
He went on to point that between June 26 and 30, last year, a grouping of residents in the area namely Mavota Gold Mining Group was trained on how to conduct mining operations and prospecting for the metal at their mining block with permit number PML 001493WLZ.
The permit was issued on February 25, last year to the grouping and it covers an area measuring 9.43 hectares around Biharamulo Gold Mine which has on the other hand provided 15 and 54 permanent and temporary jobs, respectively.
Mr Kalemani noted as well that groups of villagers from Mavota and Mkunkwa have reaped a total of 200m/- between July 2014 and December 2015, through supply of food supplies such as sweet potatoes, cassava, fruits and vegetables to the mine.

Original_Search_Title: Ankara Press to Release Novels On Val's Day
Headline: Nigeria: Ankara Press to Release Novels On Val's Day
Body_Text:
Romance publisher, Ankara Press, is set to release print format of its six debut novels and two new works in electronic format on February 14 Valentine's Day.
The six novels published in 2014 to some acclaim are 'A Tailor-Made Romance' by Oyindamola Affinnih, 'Black Sparkle Romance' by Amara Nicole Okolo, 'A Taste of Love' by Sifa Gowon, 'Love's Persuasion' by Ola Awonubi, 'Finding Love Again' by Chioma Iwunze-Ibiam and 'Elevator Kiss' by Amina Thula.
Ankara Press publishing director, Emma Shercliff, who made this known in a release, stated that it was necessitated by the fantastic response received and "enquiries from readers asking when we will be publishing the books in hard copy."
In addition to this, Ankara Press will be publishing two new novels in electronic format on Valentine's Day. 'The Seeing Place' by Aziza Eden Walker' and 'Love Next Door' by Amina Thula.
Ankara Press, an imprint of Cassava Republic, was launched in December 2014.

Original_Search_Title: MP Out to Fight Child Marriages
Headline: Tanzania: MP Out to Fight Child Marriages
Body_Text:
Pangani — Campaigning against distasteful traditions and customs that have been ingrained in communities for years does not seem to be a very tasteful way to start life as a new Member of Parliament, but the new youthful Pangani Legislator wants to give it a chance.
Worried over what seems to be a chronic problem of early marriages in the district, the new MP on a Chama Cha Mapinduzi (CCM) ticket, Mr Jumaa Aweso, wants to take a bull by the horns.
To most men, marrying a young wife translates into prestige, but Aweso wants to fight against the out-dated tradition to enable schoolgirls to continue with studies.
"I have plans to initiate a programme similar to the one initiated in Handeni District by former district commissioner Muhingo Rweyemamu," Awesso said.
The programme dubbed 'Niache Nisome' -- literally translated as 'Let me Study' -- saved girls from the scourge of early marriages, allowing them to move ahead academically.
Statistics of the programme prove his case, as 24 out of 609 Form Four students in Pangani District did not sit for the 2012 national examinations.
Their absence was described as painful to the district rated as backward in terms of education in the country with absentees coming from five out of six schools in the area.
Although some officials hesitate to point out exact cause when consulted, acting district community development officer Patricia Kinyange said husbands abandoning child mothers was to blame, as the infamous habit constituted most of the complaints she attended to in his office
"It is painful to see a 15-year-old girl becoming a mother when she is supposed to be in school. We need strategies to do away with this culture," she said.
Ms Kinyange was speaking at a prize giving ceremony for Village Multi-sectoral Aids Committees (VMACs) which a Pangani-based organisation for pioneering creative and innovative interventions, Uzikwasa, had conducted.
With a highest population of the young people on the planet than ever before, child marriage is becoming a human rights violation that the world must end if it is to achieve a fairer future for all, Aweso argues.
"The district needs to allow more girls to complete school for them to compete with boys in the job market," she added.
The Handeni campaign Mr Rweyemamu adopted aimed at pursuing a practical approach in solving the dual challenge of early pregnancies and child marriages.
The district authorities sent social welfare officers on door-to-door missions of selling merits of protecting rights of girls to reluctant parents.
The campaign did not let negligent parents off the hook; participation was mandatory and targeted students and their parents in the district. The goal was to protect rights of the girl child and to improve the quality of education in Handeni.
The awareness campaign went hand in hand with pregnancy testing across secondary schools in the district.
Aweso's crusade is not an isolated affair, but rather is a worldwide challenge. According to a global partnership known as 'Girls, Not Brides', every minute, 28 girls are married off too soon, endangering their personal development and well-being.
Bringing together over 550 civil society organisations from over 70 countries, the partnership committed to end child marriage and enable girls to fulfil their potential estimates.
Each year, 15 million are married as children before the age of 18 and thereby denied of their rights to health, education, and opportunity; and robbed of their childhood.
Fuelled by poverty, social and cultural norms; child marriage persists across countries. For many families, it is a perceived economic need -- one less mouth to feed.
Long-held beliefs and traditions based on gender inequality often mean that becoming a wife and mother is a daughter's only choice.
"Millions of girls and women already suffer the consequences of child marriage," the global partnership warns, adding: "If we do nothing, by 2030, an estimated 15.4 million girls a year will marry as children, and by 2050, the number of women married as children will grow to 1.2 billion, with devastating consequences for girls, their families and their countries."
Tanzania is among 20 countries in the world that have been practicing early marriages where, on average, two out of every five girls are married before reaching 18 years.
The 1971 Marriage Act allows girls as young as 14, albeit with special permission, and 15-year-old girls to marry.
The new legislator, who opted for politics immediately after he graduated chemistry from a university, agreed that although its level differed among 14 wards of the district, the problem was serious.
"It is more serious in some of the wards because of the influence of traditions and customs while it is almost absent in others," he said, attributing the problem to pursuit of old habits, traditions, and customs.
Ms Kinyange, in turn, attributed the problem to mind-sets, saying traditionally, marrying a young girl was prestigious to some men. Community members regarded such men as wealthier.
Letting girls continue with education appeared a waste of time and resources to some parents.
She highly commended Uzikwasa for enabling VMACs, which are almost non-existent in some areas of the country, to be active. "These committees could be awareness creators and watchdogs against such disdainful marriages," Ms Kinyange said.
The youthful legislator could perhaps take a cue by cooperating with Uzikwasa and using the committees for his proposed campaign, as they are closest organs to the people.
Apart from maintaining its programme for strengthening VMACs, Uzikwasa has been applying drama and documentary films, including the famous Fimbo ya Baba, to portray effects of marrying off young girls to wealthy old men.
In one of the Fimbo ya Baba film, a girl, who was married off to a wealthy man, ends up dying of Aids.
The district commissioner, Ms Regina Chonjo, shares the new legislator's concern. At the opening, the film portrays some of the worst forms of gender violence such as gang rape.
In the third of a series of the films on gender violence, Aisha says the episode shows how parents were irresponsible and the source of violation of women's and girls' rights through extreme instances such as gang rape.
Chonjo, who promises to take punitive action against parents and other people engaged in violating girls' rights, says there were several incidents of early marriages and child labour, happening right in the district, calling on residents to speak out and take action against them.
Chonjo lashes out at the use of girl child for selling various commodities, saying the attitude denied them of the chance for attending school and that it triggered early sex and marriages.
"A few days ago, I came across a girl who was carrying a bowl of roast cassava and when I asked her why she was engaged in that work, she said she would not be allowed to eat if she refused to do such a chore," Chonjo narrated.
Governments committed themselves to the overall protection of children and young people aged below 18 in the Convention on the Rights of the Child (CRC); however, child marriage and a range of rights implications substantially infringe the convention.
"Nations feel the impact, for child marriage drains countries of the innovation and potentials that would enable young women to thrive," the youthful legislator said.

Original_Search_Title: Two Months Down the Road, Passenger Service Train Has a Long Way to Go
Headline: Uganda: Two Months Down the Road, Passenger Service Train Has a Long Way to Go
Body_Text:
On a chilly Monday morning, I I am out of bed at 5 am earlier than usual, urged on by the nerves of an anxious day that lies ahead.
I have a scheduled train journey. Nervous at the prospect of my maiden train ride, I shudder at the sight of endless cabins snaking through the early morning fog.
The boda boda ride -my usual form of transport-which I took to the Namanve station has done little to calm my nerves.
As the train is re-fuelled at the station, passengers pay their fares and take seats. I also pay before finding my way into one of the coaches. Unsure of when it sets off, the honking is a clear sign that departure time is upon us. The nerves start cooling down.
The smile on Angelina Agudo's face is testimony that she was equally excited at the prospect of a maiden train ride. "Finally, I am here, "she sighs, as she takes her seat. Agudo, a police officer stationed in Kireka, boarded at the Kireka port. She had missed the ride on several occasions because its departure time clashes with the time she leaves for work. She lives in Nsambya and it was her first time to board a train. She usually boards a taxi or pioneer bus which charges the Shs1,500 fare.
Perceptions
Though the passenger train has been in service for one and a half months, some passengers still regard it an old-fashioned mode of transport but many are excited at the novelty it offers in a city beset with public transport problems.
Agudo speaks glowingly of the services of the train as comfortable and time-saving. "This train is far better than a taxi. I can sit anywhere I feel like and it's clean and well-aerated".
The passenger service train has five coaches. At capacity, it carries 1,000 passengers. Each coach is purposed to carry 200 passengers. Emmanuel Gonza Mugema, the team leader at Uganda Railways says the peak of boarding time is at around 6.30 am as passengers head to work.
"Passenger turn up is usually high at 6.30am when most workers and businessmen are going to their places of work and business premises respectively, "Gonza says.
The ride
A stroll through the coaches reveals comfort. Scarcely populated with two to five passengers per coach, the comfort is a far cry from packed city taxis. Ramathan Sewanyana, an undertaker at the station, says the train sets off at 6.30am for its first shift. On a bad day, it takes about 20 passengers.
Similarly, the return journey at 8.30am to Namanve has few passengers.
They are usually not more than 20 passengers. "There are not so many people who use the train from Kampala at 7.30am," says Geoffrey Mukisa, a security guard with Uganda Railways.
During the second shift at 4pm, very few people, about 10 boarded the train back to Namboole, Kireka and Kampala, probably because majority are still at work.
However, the return journey from Kampala at 5.30pm had a great turn up. Gonza says between 120 to 300 passengers board the train back to Namanve.
"There is a long stretch of over 25 kilometres from Kampala to Mukono. The evenings are worse as traffic entering the city and leaving is stiffer. That is the jam that the train breaks - and also back from work at around 5.30 pm. This is why the numbers are high," he says.
Service
The train has a number of people or administrators who operate it. It has an on board supervisor to ensure that everything is in order, a driver, security, a team leader, and ticketing clerks who are in charge of selling tickets.
Maintenance
The coaches were impeccably clean. I later learnt they are cleaned twice a day (every morning and evening).The train is serviced monthly at Nalukolongo Service Station.
Time
"In the morning, a number of vehicles; fuel tankers, heavy trailers, and various buses, taxis, and private cars fight for space on the narrow two-lane highway into the city. This forces some people to use the train," says Gonza, the team leader.
Currently, there are two shifts in a day. The train leaves Namanve station at 6.30 am daily and takes about 50 minutes to reach its destination, the Rift Valley Railways (RVR) section at Nasser Road in Kampala. It leaves Kampala at 7.30am and parks at the station in Namanve until 4pm when it leaves for the second trip.
A number of passengers board it. Most of these are corporates, according to the team leader at Uganda railways. Most of these come from Mukono, Seeta and Bweyogerere to board the train".
Sylvester Nyanzi, a passenger, shares, "I live in Bweyogerere and usually board the train from the station at 6.30 am because I have to be at work at 7.30am. Travelling by the train helps me dodge the traffic jam and I'm assured of being at work within 50 minutes".
Passengers are dropped off at three ports; Namboole, Kireka, Nakawa and the final destination which is the Rift Valley Railways (RVR) station in Kampala. In total, from Namanve to Kampala, the train takes seven minutes to reach Namboole port, another seven to reach Kireka port, 15 minutes to reach Nakawa and another 15 minutes to reach Kampala from Nakawa.
At times, the train departure for its second shift is delayed at Namanve station by about 20 minutes or more. This is due to the long process of detaching the head from the last coach to the front which takes about 20 minutes and sometimes a delay in relaying instructions due to network breakdown or slow network from the Nairobi office which controls the train through Global Positioning System (GPS).
The cost
The ticket fare from the station in Namanve to Kampala is Shs1,500. The Passengers alighting at the Namboole and Kireka port part with an affordable amount of Shs500, and the ticket fare to Nakawa is Shs1,000. These fares, may however, appear a costly venture for some passengers who think the train is supposed to be a cheaper means of transport.
"I thought the train was cheaper than taxis. I expected a train ticket to cost at most Shs1,000," says Agudo.
On the other hand, Gonza says plans are underway to reduce the fair. "During one of the meetings last week, it was proposed that tickets for Nakawa, Kireka and Namboole ports go for Shs500 and those for Kampala cost Shs 700 before close of this month. They may charge Shs1,000 to Kampala".
Train ticketing clerks are in charge of selling tickets to passengers. The money realised from ticket sales is signed out and given to the financial controller every evening, who deposits it on the account.
Reactions
Some people still look at the train as a 'new thing'. Their work will come to a standstill as they gaze at the train snake its way through.
Challenges
The railway line is used by both imports and exports cargo wagons and the passenger service train.
"This is an inconvenience as handling the two gets complicated, especially when you have to keep time," says Gonza adding that sometimes they have to wait for the engine from Kampala before the train can leave Namanve station.
The RVR has 20 per cent of their railway in Kampala. They are therefore in charge of the passenger train. The passenger service is a one year pilot study. Gonza says if the public is not responsive as expected, then it will be suspended.
RVR is responsible for keeping the train in good condition, ensuring that time is kept and repairing and maintaining the train and Uganda Railways (UR) is in charge of running the train.
A number of people have not yet bought the passenger service idea. The majority are not used to passenger trains.
The toilets in the train are not functional; the argument is that the current distance being travelled by passengers is short.
There are no security kits for checking. Passengers enter and exit freely.
Ritah Nakitende, a businesswoman suggests that departure time, especially, the 4 o'clock shift, be improved upon and that management considers adding more trains and shifts or trips so that people who can't keep time can travel at will.
Prospects
Currently, the route being plied is Kampala to Namanve. With time, the train will go as far as Busega and Mukono.
The number of trips will be increased to three and shorter coaches are going to be imported. These will come in with a number of privileges such as; tea service and wireless internet.
Also, classes will be introduced. "We shall have the first, second and third class travellers," highlights Gonza.
Business impact
Notably, there is a relatively lucrative business atmosphere at the Namanve train station. A number of food vendors and retail shop attendants start business as early as 5.00am. They sell snacks such as; cassava chips, samosas, 'rolex', and all kinds of eats including tea. They target early risers who wait to board the train without breakfast from home, plus the staff who live in the quarters at the station.
Unfortunately, other people neigbouring the railway line are not a contented lot. They live and operate at the mercy of Kampala City Council Authority (KCCA). "I have been here for 15 years. I don't use the train because KCCA chased us from here because of this train. We are operating here illegally. Besides, my work doesn't necessitate me using a train. I cannot pay my money to enter the train because it doesn't even stop anywhere you want to alight like taxis and buses," says Byamukama Kizito, a businessman at the station and washing bay owner.

Original_Search_Title: Kwara Assembly Approves N2.7 Billion Loan for Salaries, Official Vehicles
Headline: Nigeria: Kwara Assembly Approves N2.7 Billion Loan for Salaries, Official Vehicles
Body_Text:
KWARA State House of Assembly yesterday approved the request of the state government to access N2.7 billion loan from Sterling Bank for the payment of October 2015 salary arrears of workers and pensioners in the 16 local councils and purchase of official vehicles for public office holders in the state.
The state government is to spend N1.5 billion of the amount on payment of salaries while N1.2 billion is for procurement of vehicles for politicians.
The approval followed requests from the state Governor, Abdulfatah Ahmed, in separate letters addressed to the Speaker, Dr. Ali Ahmad, dated 11th and 13th January 2016 respectively read at the floor of the House by the Deputy Speaker, Elder Mathew Okedare.
Leading other members in the debate, the Leader of the House, Alhaji Hassan Oyeleke, explained that the approval for the loan facilities became imperative in view of the dwindling revenue allocation from the Federation Account.
Oyeleke added that payment of salaries to workers was sacrosanct to make them perform optimally just as he argued that provision of official vehicles was equally necessary to enhance the performance of legislators.
Other members who contributed to the debate expressed confidence that the loan facility if judiciously used would address the ongoing industrial dispute by teachers at the basic school level.
However, member representing Ojomu/Balogun constituency, Mr. Saheed Popoola and member representing Kaiama, Kemanji, Wajibe constituency, Alhaji Ahmed Ibn Mohammed, urged the state government to look inward by evolving ways to improve the state's Internally Generated Revenue to stop overdependence on the Federation Account.
Meanwhile, the demand for Garri a staple food processed from cassava tuber has fallen in Ilorin, the state capital.A visit by The Guardian to three major Garri processing centres in Ilorin showed that demand for the food has fallen by over 60 percent.
This may be connected the outbreak of Lassa fever in some states and warnings to people by health officials to avoid the food for now.
At Lanjorin, Sabo-Oke area of the town, dealers in the food who before now realise up to N30,000 daily say they hardly make a sale of N18,000. The situation is the same at Eleboto area of Sango where the traders complain of lack of buyers.
However, most of the traders are not aware of the reason for the sudden decline in the demand for the product. Mrs. Ibukun Oladunjoye at Lanjorin said, "we just discovered that sales dropped in the last few days, but we thought it could be scarcity of funds."
Some residents now prefer cats to rat traps to keep rodents away from their homes. This has led to an increase in the price of domestic cats to between N2,000 and N2,500.

Original_Search_Title: Small Efforts, Big Changes - Business Training Turns Around Women's Lives
Headline: Rwanda: Small Efforts, Big Changes - Business Training Turns Around Women's Lives
Body_Text:
When I visited their training class at Indego Africa in Kacyiru, the women from different co-operatives around the city were oblivious of my presence as they listened attentively to their computer and English language trainers.
The training by facilitators from Indego Africa, a nonprofit social enterprise, seeks to empower the women with skills to better run their businesses and improve their living standards. The organization has helped women like Emerienne Nyiramana, a resident of Kicukiro District and a Primary Six dropout, to find their footing in the entrepreneurship world.
Though Nyiramana, who dropped out of school over 20 years ago, could not achieve her childhood dream of becoming a teacher, she has used skills acquired from Indego Africa training programmes to improve her life and support fellow women.
"I dropped out of school when I was going to start secondary school education. Though I tried out different things to earn money, nothing seemed to work for me," she says.
Nyiramana says later, she decided to enroll for a tailoring course in 2005 to improve her livelihood following previous failed attempts.
After the course, Nyiramana says she joined COCOKI co-operative in 2007. This was to be her turning point.
"When we started working together, I was happy because I knew that one day I will become a tailoring tutor and fulfill my dream," she says.
But challenges, such as lack of accounting skills, and business management and lack of market for the group's products, as well as the fact that most of the co-operative members were all illiterate, affected her efforts.
However, luck was on her side as Indego Africa was ready to partner with and equip the women with various skills, like business management, English classes and using computers.
The partnership has proved pivotal for the group and others in Kigali and different parts of the country as the skills acquired from the many training programmes have helped them operate profitably and search better markets for their products.
"We are now able to balance our books using spread sheets on computers. Filing tax returns and business records is now easy, thanks to the bookkeeping and accounting acquired from the training," she says. She adds that now she is the group's treasurer and co-ordinate's the co-operative's marketing strategy, linking them to the market.
The co-operative makes men and women clothes, earrings, notebook covers, men's ties and bowties, necklaces, bangles, handbags, and laptop bags, which they sell in Kigali and abroad. Other products are sold from Ikaze showroom in Kicukiro. The group is made of about 200 women artisans.
Nyiramana says they used to earn about Rwf20,000 before, but their earnings have since risen to between Rwf50,000 and Rwf100,000 each, after deducting 10 per cent savings. Nyiramana's fortunes continued to increase and she was selected to go to the US for training to improve her competencies.
She says she is now a trainer for various women co-operatives, and has set up her own tailoring workshop, where she employs two workers.
Beata Uwamwiza, one of the other member of the co-operative, have been able to enhance their living standards, thanks to the organisation's intervention. Uwamwiza says she started tailoring in 2004 and joined the co-operative four years later in 2008. Uwamwiza says the co-operative has attracted more clients since they were able to improve product quality and provide good customer service. This, she adds, has greatly boosted their savings, noting that with new skills, the group's activities are now done faster and efficiently. "We have embraced the savings culture and no longer use manual accounting since we are now computer literate," she says.
"I have managed to educate my five children, three of whom have completed secondary school, while the two are going to join Senior Six this term," she adds.
Uwamwiza says, she pays over Rwf55,000 per term for the three children attending public school, while she parts with Rwf120,000 per term for those in private school. "I have been able to afford this because now I can make better products that sale at good prices... I am planning to build a permanent residential house using savings from my tailoring job," she says.
Aisha Niyotwagira from Ububoshyibwa Gaseke co-operative says she has been able to save, thanks to improved earnings. She says the group sells some of the products to international buyers with the help of Indego Africa, which links them to outside markets. "I have joined a SACCO and saved some money from which I have bought four small pieces of lands, where I grow cassava," Niyotwagira says.
Challenges
The women artisans have made significant gains over the past few years. However, they still face numerous challenges, like lack of raw materials, which are holding back their progress. "We lack raw materials like 'inshinge' (a grass species used to make baskets)... we buy the materials we use currently from Northern Province, which is costly," Nyiramana says.
Indego Africa also trains the women in other skills, including business management, leadership, marketing, as well as communication skills and customer care. The organisation works with 30 women artisan groups involved in handicraft making to help them improve their skills and hence their operations and products. They believe a business-driven approach is essential to African women's empowerment.

Original_Search_Title: Neighbours Recall Aine's Last Hours At His Home
Headline: Uganda: Neighbours Recall Aine's Last Hours At His Home
Body_Text:
Kampala — Christopher Aine, the head of security in Go-Forward, the outfit through which former prime minister Amama Mbabazi is running for president, went missing in the third week of December last year after a violent encounter between Mr Mbabazi's bodyguards and NRM supporters in Ntungamo.
His family believe he is dead-- allegedly after being tortured by security operatives who reportedly picked him from his house in Kyanja, a Kampala suburb.
Police have, more than once, said they don't, and have never had him. They believe he is in hiding and a Shs20 million bounty has been put on his head by the police.
President Museveni told a press briefing recently that Aine was in hiding, a claim Mr Mbabazi chided during the live presidential debate saying: "If you know where someone is, why not pick him?"
Thirty days since Aine is believed to have been picked by security operatives, Sunday Monitor visited his home in Kyanja, interacted with the neighbours and other village members, the local police and village leadership. The more the interactions went on, the more questions popped up.
Kyanja is a quiet hilly suburb, about 10km north of Kampala. It is covered in lush green.
A dusty potholed unpaved road picks on from the tarmac road at Kyanja Trading Centre and leading through the village. It is off that dusty road in Walufumbe Zone that Aine rented a gated apartment.
The apartment has two doors--one leading to Aine's house and the other to where a couple, the only people in Aine's gate, live. Aine's love for Mr Mbabazi is not lost; at his door lies a torn campaign poster of the former premier.
In front of Aine's apartment is a bigger house. The two share the outer wall fence and are only separated by a thick wall inside. The house is near the main village road. Around 10 strides and you will, through a bushy footpath, be at his gate.
A community police post is just 200 metres away and at a quarter that distance is Mama Sara's kiosk.
Every morning, at 5am, she says, she sits in there frying cassava to sell to early risers. She also doubled as Aine's laundress. Across the road and overlooking Aine's house are shops and a makeshift lot for night parking.
The neighbours, although confirming they saw police at Aine's house on the morning of December 16, they say the police did not pick him from his house, as earlier claimed by his brother.
Aine's immediate neighbour says police knocked on Aine's door and windows for about 15 minutes but he never opened.
"They were three men in plain clothes and they first knocked at my door mistaking it to be Aine's," she said. "They started knocking at his (Aine) and even at the back window as they called out his name but he just kept quiet and after 15 minutes they left," the neighbour said.
She is convinced Aine was in the house because she had heard someone in his house flashing the toilet and shutting doors.
She describes Aine as a friendly neighbour who always inquired about how she was doing. He once helped replace her faulty light bulb. "I never heard a sound in his house even after those who were knocking left and it has been like that since. I hope he is well, wherever he is," she said.
Before the visit, Aine's cousin, Ezra Kabugo, who swore an affidavit in support of a writ of habeas corpus application in court (a court order to produce a suspect from police custody) -- to be heard on January 27, had narrated how police went to his house looking for Aine and on calling to notify him, he had confirmed hearing people knocking at his door and movement in his compound.
This corroborates the neighbour's version that Aine was in his house when the police raided.
Mama Sara says that on the day police came, Aine gave her clothes to wash and make sure they dried and got pressed before sunset.
"He gave me the clothes at 9am and told me he wanted them to be worked on very fast because he was going somewhere,' she said. "But he has never come back and I still have the clothes in my house."
She comes off as a shy woman, speaking with a shy smile, looking more at the ground and avoiding another meeting thereafter.
Two days before his disappearance, Aine had withdrawn Shs190,000 from a local mobile money agent.
"He spoke on phone throughout the process and sounded like something was bothering him. I never paid too much attention to him, but he kept telling the person on the line: 'I am coming' and spoke a mix of English and Runyankore," the agent said adding that it was the first time Aine was using her services.
A neighbour, who says he is one of the few people in Kyanja that Aine freely talked to also doubts that Aine was picked from his residence. He says Aine was not the type who could simply be picked without making a scene.
Looking through Aine's glass door, a couple of shoes and a plastic carpet cleaner can be seen strewn on the floor.
No one, the neighbours say, has ever opened Aine's house since and none of them has ever been interrogated by police, not even the village local council chairman. There is also no poster of the wanted Aine anywhere in the village.
"It is very unusual that a person can get lost and police look for him and yet we as the local leaders are not even asked," said Mr David Nsubuga, the village chairman.
"Now I am going to take full interest in this issue now that people like you have started asking."
Another neighbour, who spoke fondly of Aine, described him as a village hero who had become a topic of discussion since his encounter with police in Jinja District last year.
On some evenings, the neighbour recounted, as he passed through the village, young men would idly gather at a reasonable distance to marvel and behold the enigma of "Amama's man" -- as they fondly referred to him.
