6. Mod-ish
Started off by two sisters and run out of their homes in the Mother City, Mod-ish designs beautiful classical modest wear items. Think long dresses, full sleeves, scarves and loose and flowy options - they've even showcased at fashion weeks! The pricing is really reasonable too. Check out their Facebook page for more.
Neckpiece : @belleviecollection Makeup : @thelipstickbullet Photographer : @waafiq
A photo posted by Mod-ish ® (@mod_.ish) on Jun 7, 2016 at 12:28pm PDT
7. Shameema Dharsey's 99 Names of Allah adult colouring book
Adult colouring books are one of the best things out there right now - they look pretty, and they're billed to be a therapeutic stress reliever. Artist and illustrator Shameema Dharsey is adding a third perk to the list: remembrance of God. This Ramadan, she's launching the first volume of the five that she hopes to release, each with 20 names inside. Featuring beautiful geometric shapes and paper that can be easily cut out and framed, we love the innovation behind this.
8. Fathima's Studio
Durban-based Fathima Kathrada has been running her own studio as a freelance graphic designer, illustrator and hand letterer since 2012, when she left her full-time job to pursue her self-employed adventure.
She does beautifully intricate hand lettering and drawings, and her aesthetic is ON POINT. She also does freelance branding and design work - check out more of her stuff on her blog.
And now for something completely different. Sometimes when you're feeling creatively stuck, as I've been, it's time do so some exploring and experimenting. Ideally we should do this all the time, but we forget. So after giving my succulents a clean up this morning and noticing how pretty the colours on the dried leaves are, I decided to make a dress ð
A photo posted by fathima (@fathimasstudio) on May 14, 2016 at 3:21am PDT
9. Haya Collective
Launched in 2015, Haya Collective is a South African-based global online store which specialises in producing understated and versatile modest wear, particularly for Muslim women. The collection features items such as abayas and scarves, but also long skirts, dresses, tops and dresses, and is set to launch a sleepwear line soon. They also offer free shipping and delivery in South Africa - just one other reason we love local.
In anticipation of the blessed month, we're excited to present our first ever Ramadan Collection. Available exclusively online. Shop new arrivals now at www.hayacollective.com
A photo posted by Haya Collective (@hayacollective) on Jun 3, 2016 at 2:55am PDT
10. Your local samoosa aunty
We all know an Aunty Hafsa/Mariam/Aisha/Choti who makes our lives easier by selling us savouries, especially around Ramadan time. Bless them for all of the hard work that they do, feeding many families across the country every night. Remember them, and good on you for empowering them by giving them your business! (And if you don't have one, I'm happy to pass on a couple of recommendations.)
A photo posted by Party Platters Cape Town (@partyplatterscapetown) on May 30, 2016 at 4:15am PDT
Happy fasting, and happy supporting local Muslim talent this month!
Are there any initiatives or cool creatives we've missed? Comment below or tweet us @thedailyvox!

Original_Search_Title: Bye-Bye Breakfast Cereal?
Headline: Kenya: Bye-Bye Breakfast Cereal?
Body_Text:
Rheumatoid arthritis runs in the family (my grandmother's hands are completely disfigured), so when Mama Parmar noticed swelling in certain joints, she was keen to get it under control.
For as long as I can remember, Mama Parmar has always started her day with a bowl of cereal. By choosing a wholegrain version with no added sugar, she felt that she was getting in valuable fibre and strengthening her bones with the calcium in the milk. Unfortunately, because of her dormant condition, she was actually harming her health with this meal.
You see, rheumatoid arthritis is what's known as an autoimmune disease. This means that that Mama Parmar's immune system had begun to attack her joints, which had resulted in inflammation. The reason that food comes into this is because this 'attack' very often begins in the gut. The body mistakes undigested food particles for invaders and starts to attack them (this is why a diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome is often seen a few years prior to that of arthritis).
Why the body then starts to attack its own tissues isn't quite understood. What we do know is that gluten (found in wheat, rye, oats and barley) and casein (found in dairy) seem to be the worst offenders. This is something that I've seen confirmed time and again at the clinic, where supposedly long-term, chronic conditions like lupus and rheumatoid arthritis have gone into remission simply with a dietary overhaul.
So aside from the removal of gluten and dairy, I suggested that Mama Parmar cut down her intake of sugar and deep-fried food, since both prime the body for inflammation, while getting in eight glasses of water a day to keep her joints well-lubricated. The last big food change was to avoid members of the nightshade family (potatoes, tomatoes, peppers, aubergines) as these can trigger flare-ups.
When we first talked about her condition, Mama Parmar was keen to take glucosamine, a popular supplement for arthritis (works by building cartilage). However, as I explained to her, it's actually a much better fit for osteoarthritis, since this is a condition where there is considerable wear-and-tear at the joints. Instead, I advised her to take fish oils, since omega-3 fatty acids are great at reducing inflammation (a supplement containing a minimum of 500-750mg of the active components EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) and DHA (docosahexaenoic acid).
What should Mama Parmar have for breakfast now that she's ditched the cereal? Starchy root vegetables like sweet potato with a boiled egg is one option, but she actually prefers a bowl of millet porridge, with a little apple and cinnamon to sweeten.

Original_Search_Title: The Magic of Brand Ambassadors
Headline: Kenya: The Magic of Brand Ambassadors
Body_Text:
So you decided you want to be a brand ambassador. Brand ambassadors increase brand awareness and encourage people to spend on your product. Being the face, spokesperson or representative appears lucrative at a glance, easy money for attractive, likeable, relatable people.
No wonder there are self-described influencers who make this a life goal. At its core, being a noteworthy brand ambassador is work.
Once identified and installed in their role, brand ambassadors work their heads off. Mainly because they are wanted for more than their influence and/or looks.
Just a few examples. When skincare brand Neutrogena approached actress Kerry Washington, she said yes - but, on condition she must have a voice in the company. A brilliant move.
Not only did Neutrogena not have products suitable for Kerry's complexion, she worked with them to develop foundation and lip colour for black women, expanding their client base into a previously non-existent demographic.
This is quite different from Lupita's role as Lancome brand ambassador where, at least according to interviews and public knowledge, is simply a face. Pop star Rihanna may be celebrated as the first black face of Dior yet the biggest cultural impact she is making is with sportswear brand Puma. Besides being the face and body, she is collaborator and designer.
This week she launched her third instalment of Puma Creepers. They sold out in 35 minutes. RiRi is famously referenced as "Puma's secret weapon against Adidas." She is countered by pro basketballer, fashion icon and owner of magnificent beard James Harden, formerly with Nike, now with Adidas after signing a $200 million deal, a relationship designed to last over a decade.
SOCIAL INFLUENCERS
It requires Harden be healthy, stay in the league and work with the Adidas team to design footwear as well as immersion in their marketing and brand strategy.
Ergo, your brand ambassador of choice must be aware of what they bring to the table, the strength of their own personal brand and the advantages collaboration brings. Your brand, their platform and their audience must have a business sweet spot.
With the expansion of celebrity, small businesses can get in on endorsements just as much as big names. Bloggers, vloggers, social media stars and media personalities with strong online presence are identified as influencers, as are fans of your brand who willingly share their experiences to celebrities with a voice, platform, influence and/or experiences with your brand. Brands now have options.
Before bringing an ambassador on board you have to be very clear about your brand. An ambassador takes cues from you. They do not do the heavy lifting. They support what you already have in place, promoting what you have so far created. They fit into an already existing structure.
Research, making a list of all possible candidates. Getting an ambassador includes acquiring their intangibles. You get their (non) work ethic and nuances of their personal brand.
Also be clear about what you want them for. Do you want their engagement online and offline with their own audience? You want interactive ambassadors who live in a manner that makes this collaboration believable. Meaning if you are a lifestyle personality, you visibly take care of your body.
You need to listen to your ambassador because part of their job is to give you feedback. These are measurables and deliverables put on contract, informing the terms and conditions. Here is the thing about long term ambassadors that brands need to understand; you cannot expect them to survive purely on freebies or quid pro quo. You have to put money on the table because 'stuff' does not pay the rent.
Aside from scouting, hiring and supervising an ambassador, have in mind an exit strategy beyond the expiration of the contract.
Tennis player Maria Sharapova endorsed premium and luxury brands Nike, Tag Heuer, Samsung, Porsche and was a United Nations Goodwill Ambassador. After a doping scandal last year she was dropped like a hot potato.
As a brand, what is your tolerance for human frailties? You need to know because being perceived as insensitive or too eager to drop an ambassador, especially one you have had a good working relationship with, can cost you brand equity.
Consider Subway, who launched a "Be Yourself" campaign, Speedo, who's brand thrived long term and Omega, who figured when the greatest Olympian ever, swimmer Michael Phelps, was snared in a marijuana scandal, and stood by him, that there is no amount of money or strategy in the world that could have ever earned them that much publicity.

Original_Search_Title: Hip-Hop Is for the Chosen Few - Paccy
Headline: Rwanda: Hip-Hop Is for the Chosen Few - Paccy
Body_Text:
Rwandan Female rapper Oda Pacifique Uzamberumwana, aka Paccy, was born in Kigali on March 6th , 1990. She studied at Gatsata Pimary School, APEMU Ruli and EAV Bigogwe, Gisenyi, for O' and A' levels respectively. She then joined KIST for one year before changing to Rwanda Tourism University College (RTUC). The eldest child in a family of three girls, the rapper joined the music industry in 2009 with her release of 'Ese Nzapfa' Her latest song is 'Igitego.' She talked to The Rwanda Focus about her career and why she chose rap/hip hop style.
The genre you are operating from is rare among female singers, why did you pick it over the rest?
There is no particular music style specified for males or females; it's all about passion, not the music genre itself.
However, I grew up watching American artists like Eminem, Dr. Dre and Eve, so I wondered why I couldn't follow in their footsteps. That's how I decided to share my message with fans through hip hop.
Why do you think there are less female artists doing hip hop or rap music?
Not everyone is obliged to sing hip hop; people sing depending on their favorite genre. However, I feel like everyone should respect hip hop because it is not an easy style.
It's not an easy genre but it's what you thought you could do best, right?
Well, there are many artists who sing other styles with ease but it is so different in hip hop. Not everyone can feel confident to rap... I don't know how I can explain it and that's why I insist hip hop deserves respect.
Besides international singers, which Rwandan artists influenced you?
There were other hip hop singers in Rwanda like Riderman, Tuff Gangz [hip hop group of Jay Polly, Bull Dogg, Fireman & P- FLA], Pacson, DMS, MC Mahoniboni and more. I started to do my music around them to take hip hop a notch higher and prove that this is a genre which deserves credit rather than slamming it as street gangster's music.
To what extent have you benefited from your music career since you do it as a profession?
Music is my business which I depend on for my everyday needs. It is a career I chose and there are lots of things that I have attained through it. There is no need to list them down but what I have so far achieved in life is because of my music. It's not too much nor is it too little. I could have stopped it if it weren't so fruitful.
What do you like to eat/drink most?
I like to eat Irish potatoes, beans, sweet potatoes and meat. I like to drink fresh milk.
What is the saddest thing that hurt you most?
The horrible tragic history of the 1994 Genocide against Tutsis always scares me. Personally, my life didn't face any extraordinary pain but life is a mixture of ups and downs.
How different are you from the Paccy of 2009?
I am totally different from the young one I was in 2009. I evolve year after year and the vision is different too. I started music with a target. I still have a target but it's now a different one since I have grown up.
What target do you have at the moment?
I want to expand my music career and reach the top in the region and continental level. You can't make your dream come true if you don't push and push, but the more you dream, the more you achieve.
Where do you think your talent comes from?
I heard from my elders that there were people who were musicians in my family before I was born. Maybe my talent has grassroots from there.
At which level do you see Rwandan music industry?
Rwandan music is evolving time after time and we will keep improving. There is hope that in two, three years... things will be amazing.
Any message for females who wish to join the music industry, especially hip hop or rap?
Some people were branding hip hop as gangster's music at some point. But it is different nowadays. The truth about the message in hip hop is well received by the audience and they now call it 'healing' music that positively changes the world. The door is open for females to continue the hip hop journey and enjoy the passion through it.

Original_Search_Title: 'Reduce Imports, Manufacture More'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'Reduce Imports, Manufacture More'
Body_Text:
BARCLAYS Bank Zimbabwe managing director George Guvamatanga says Zimbabweans should go through a paradigm shift and focus more on manufacturing instead of importing products that can be produced in the country. This would reduce the import bill which reached U.S.$6 billion last year and in the process alleviate pressure on cash.Reduced demand for cash would mean that there will not be cash shortage as being experienced currently.
In a keynote address at the Barclays/ Building Opportunities on Student Talent (BOOST) Partnership Launch on Wednesday, Mr Guvamatanga told young entrepreneurs that Zimbabweans are importing products that are available locally, putting further strain on liquidity in the country.
Zimbabwe is currently facing cash shortages as a result of over reliance on imports compared to fewer exports, a situation that has led to cash shortages.
"We are having cash shortages because we are a nation that it is consuming more than it is producing. If you are importing more than you are exporting it means that as a country you are spending more money than what you have.
"So if you imports are $6 billion, that is what our imports were in Zimbabwe in 2015 and exports of $3 billion, it means that you have a shortage of cash.
"But what exactly are we importing? We are importing everything from cars, expensive whisky, Blue Label, Black Label, expensive wine and we even go to the extent of importing water. But that's not the worst of it all," said Mr Guvamatanga.
In a bid to plug the importation of locally available products, the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe, working with bankers and industry has drawn up a priority list for imports.
This list prioritises products that are not locally available, this to discourage shipping out of money unnecessarily.
Instead, business should be looking at manufacturing most of the products that are pushing up imports.
"From a business perspective we should be thinking of manufacturing the things that we are spending $6 billion on. If you go to the retail outlets today you will see imported sweet potatoes and vegetables," he said.
Barclays Bank Zimbabwe officially handed over $68 197,50 to the BOOST Fellowship organisation for the Business Incubation Project aimed at helping young business entrepreneurs to prosper. The project will provide business incubation services for youth SME owners so that their businesses and ideas can be accelerated and systematised, thereby creating successful enterprises.
The young entrepreneurs are provided with a comprehensive and integrated range of support, including, but not limited to business support services, clustering and networking opportunities as well as technical services and finance raising strategies.
The programme will encourage and empower youths to take advantage of emerging opportunities in technology to ignite and develop innovative ideas that solve real social challenges thereby developing a culture of local solutions for local problems.
Mr Guvamatanga challenged the young entrepreneurs to investigate what is being imported and then manufacture those products to fill the gap.
"What I urge you to do is to go into the shops and compile a list of all the products that we are importing which we can make locally, that's your business opportunity," said Mr Guvamatanga.

Original_Search_Title: Army Kills 58 Boko Haram Militants
Headline: Nigeria: Army Kills 58 Boko Haram Militants
Body_Text:
The Nigerian Army said yesterday troops of the 153 Task Force Brigade and 5 Brigade killed 58 Boko Haram militants during an encounter in Musari village of Borno State on Monday.
A statement from army spokesman, Colonel Sani Usman, said a soldier died in the operation by the troops who were clearing remaining Boko Haram camps in Musari area.
He added that 52 motorcycles, several bags of foodstuff such as beans, onions, sweet potatoes and guinea corn as well as cartons of fish were recovered.
Usman also stated that troops of the 29 Task Force Brigade conducting operations on the remnants of Boko Haram terrorists, cleared Abukarti, Baradili, Borgozo, Gaijaribul Abukar, Kolomi, Marguba, Umarumi and Yasori I and II villages in Borno State.

Original_Search_Title: Boko Haram - Troops Kill 58 Terrorists, Lose a Soldier
Headline: Nigeria: Boko Haram - Troops Kill 58 Terrorists, Lose a Soldier
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Nigerian Army said, yesterday, that it lost one of its soldiers and killed no fewer than 58 terrorists, following a clearance operation to rid insurgents from their hideout at Musari.
The Army in statement signed by Acting Director, Army Public Relations, Col. Sani Usman, said: "In continuation with the clearance of the remnants of the Boko Haram terrorists, troops of 153 Task Force Brigade, 5 Brigade, Monday, had an encounter with terrorists at Musari village.
"During the clearance operations, a soldier paid the supreme price, while the troops killed 58 terrorists and recovered two hand grenades, 52 motorcycles, and several bags of foodstuffs such as beans, onions, sweet potatoes and guinea corn, as well as cartons of fish."
Meanwhile, the Police Service Commission, yesterday, approved the promotion of 156 senior police officers to the next rank, including one Assistant Inspector General of Police, 10 Commissioners of Police and 19 Deputy Commissioners of Police.
The new AIG is Musa Katsina Mohammed, the Commissioner of Police, Kano State, while the new commissioners of police include Ero Oga; Yunana Y. Babas; Dan-Malam Mohammed; Mu'azu Zubairu Halilu; Rabiu Yusuf; Sanusi Nma Lemu; Ahmed Iliyasu; Isyaku Muktar Garko; Mohammed Uba Kura and Alhassan Mamuda.

Original_Search_Title: Troops Clear More Boko Haram Camps, Kill 58
Headline: Nigeria: Troops Clear More Boko Haram Camps, Kill 58
Body_Text:
The Nigerian army on Tuesday said that troops of 153 Task Force Brigade and 5 Brigade killed 58 terrorists during an encounter with Boko Haram on Monday in Musari village, Borno State.
A statement by army spokesman Colonel Sani Usman said a soldier died in the operation by the troops clearing remaining Boko Haram camps in Musari area.
He stated that: "during the clearance operations, a soldier paid the supreme price, while the troops killed 58 terrorist and recovered 2 hand grenades."
He added that, 52 motorcycles, several bags of foodstuffs such as beans, onions, sweet potatoes and guinea corn, as well as cartons of fish was recovered.
Usman also stated that troops of the 29 Task Force Brigade conducting operations on the remnants of Boko Haram terrorists, cleared: Abukarti, Baradili, Borgozo, Gaijaribul Abukar, kolomi, Marguba, Umarumi and Yasori I and II villages in Borno state.
He added that: "The gallant troops also recovered several items including one single barrel gun, motorcycles, drums and bags of millet and dead cattle that ran into an Improvised Explosive Device (IED).

Original_Search_Title: Troops Kill Boko Haram Insurgents, Cut Terrorists' Logistics - Army
Headline: Nigeria: Troops Kill 58 Boko Haram Insurgents, Cut Terrorists' Logistics  - Army
Body_Text:
Troops of the Nigerian Army on Monday killed at least 58 Boko Haram terrorists and seized arms and foodstuff being ferried to the group, the Army has said.
The Acting Director, Army Public Relations, Sani Usman, said in a statement made available to PREMIUM TIMES Tuesday that the terrorists were killed by troops of the Army's 153 Task Force Brigade and 5 Brigade who executed a clearance operation at the Musari area of Borno State.
"In continuation with the clearance of the remnants of the Boko Haram terrorists, troops of 153 Task Force Brigade, 5 Brigade on Monday had an encounter with terrorists at Musari village on Monday," Mr. Usman, a colonel said in the statement.
"During the clearance operations, a soldier paid the supreme price, while the troops killed 58 terrorist and recovered 2 hand grenades, 52 motorcycles, several bags of foodstuffs such as beans, onions, sweet potatoes and guinea corn, as well as cartons of fish."
News of the terrorists' death came shortly before the Army announced the "remarkable success" its 29 Task Force Brigade was making in another clearance operation aimed at smoking out more insurgents from their hideouts.
"Within the last two days the units under the Brigade cleared the remnants of Boko Haram terrorists hibernating in the following areas; Abukarti, Baradili, Borgozo, Gaijaribul Abukar, kolomi, Marguba, Umarumi and Yasori 1 and 2," Mr. Usman said.
"The gallant troops also recovered several items including one single barrel gun, motorcycles, drums and bags of millet and dead cattle that ran into Improvised Explosive Device (IED) buried by the Boko Haram terrorists.
"Similarly, the troops detonated an IED in one of the buildings at Yasori 1. It was equally discovered that prior to the clearance, the terrorists have also been frequenting Borgozo general area which they used as harbour and staging area when conducting their nefarious activities."

Original_Search_Title: Pablo - When Love Gets Lost Along the Way
Headline: Uganda: Pablo - When Love Gets Lost Along the Way
Body_Text:
I have noticed that before marriage, some couples call each other sweet nothings, but after marriage, nothing sweet.
What used to be saved in the phonebook as 'Honey' has been replaced with 'Police.' What used to be a smooth conversation while driving home on a date has graduated into a Q & A session on your way to pick your children from school.
I guess when you get married, the naked truth about you is exposed and it is hard to deal with, especially if one partner wants to run the show. What started off as marital art slowly becomes martial arts.
There are a few things you need to do in order to continuously enjoy your relationship with your partner. And I am not talking about a business partner here or, rather, prostitute. Some folks argue that since prostitution is a business, then the prostitutes would obviously be described as your business partners.
The difference between an orthodox business partner and a prostitute is that the former, after striking a deal, will always say, "It was a pleasure doing business with you." But the latter will always say, "It was business doing pleasure with you." But let's keep that for another day.
I have realised that you don't have to take a relationship extremely seriously for it to thrive. It also needs some breathing space to have fun. I was told of a couple that was on a date and however much the lady tried to lighten up the man with jokes, his face got more stern instead.
The lady then asked the man what was disturbing him and he said nothing. The lady was forced to ask him, "So, if there is nothing disturbing you, how come you aren't laughing at my jokes?" The man replied, "I thought at the beginning of the dinner you mentioned that you wanted a serious relationship." That kind of seriousness that can't appreciate a joke is a recipe for disaster.
I have also learnt that you have to know what your partner 'needs,' not only what they 'want.' I witnessed a scenario on Valentine's day when a man earned himself a flowery beating from his wife. He wanted to surprise her with a bouquet of flowers, and upon delivery the wife asked what the flowers had cost.
She was bitter with him for wasting money worth two sacks of Irish potatoes from the village. The wife grabbed the flowers and used them to slap sense into him. If only he had asked what she really needed, then he wouldn't be in a flowery mess.
Tell the truth at all times. Yes, it will make you mad but it will set you free. The beauty about the truth is that it will always show up when you least expect it. A story is told of a couple that had three sons. The day the government announced that it was going to pay for four children in every household, the husband opened up and unashamedly declared that he had had an extra-marital affair that resulted into the birth of a baby girl.
The wife's silence was so loud that the husband's guilty conscience manifested in his allegations that she gave him only three boys so he had to look beyond their matrimonial bed to get a girl.
The wife told him to go and bring the child since there was nothing she could change. When the husband returned with his daughter, he found the wife at home with only one son and asked to know where the rest had gone since he wanted to introduce their sister to them.
The lady unashamedly told him, "do you think that you are the only one who heard the announcement? Their fathers came and took them." Their marriage has never been the same again.

Original_Search_Title: Johnnie Walker Walks Through Fort Portal
Headline: Uganda: Johnnie Walker Walks Through Fort Portal
Body_Text:
Mountains Of The Moon hotel in Fort Portal was a hub of activity on Saturday as its proprietor, William Byaruhanga, hosted the Johnnie Walker Symphony In Blue private whiskey tasting dinner.
Byaruhanga, also a city lawyer and businessman, invited his contemporaries from as far as Kampala as well as areas within and around Fort Portal. The event was a continuation of the series of events organized by Uganda Breweries for its exclusive Blue Club members to which the host belongs.
One of the key moments of the events is always the mentoring. A whiskey ambassador guides the guest on how to appreciate and savour the drink. Tasked with this job was Capital FM's Marcus Kwikiriza who is getting better with every event he mentors.
Diageo could be breeding a future global ambassador for the brand, the best job if like Marcus, you love your whiskey. After the blue-carpet moments and photo sessions for the elegantly- dressed guests for the black tie event, they were ushered into the cocktail area for a few minutes of mingling before being ushered into the main hall immaculately set up by Fenon Events.
Following welcome remarks from Byaruhanga, the starter - stuffed avocado salad - was served.
With the salad plates cleared, the Johnnie Walker Platinum Label was served - not to be hastily tossed back in a quick shot, but to be 'nosed' for its distinct flavours, twirled in the mouth and finally swallowed to enjoy the finish.
It went down well with the marinated aged T-bone steak served with mashed potato, creamed spinach and steamed vegetables. The sweet, smooth to smoky finish Gold Label Reserve was served with the dessert before the long awaited moment.
Beautiful ushers dressed in blue crossed the room on to the stage. The lights went out and three men appeared with a bottle of one of the whiskey makers' pinnacle offerings: Johnnie Walker Blue Label.
The presentation was well-choreographed, and receiving a round of applause from the guests.
With the tasting done, guests purchased some bottles on offer; some were admitted into the Blue Club and the real drinking officially kicked off.
The party went on till past 4am with DJ Moustey keeping the guests on the dance floor all night.

Original_Search_Title: 'I'm Single, Preparing to Start Searching'
Headline: Uganda: 'I'm Single, Preparing to Start Searching'
Body_Text:
We don't see you in public anymore. What's up?
I am not like the musicians who have to be everywhere. I am a sportsman who only makes news when I have a fight. But I am also busy meditating about different things.
Like what? What exactly is Golola meditating on?
Moses Golola is busy thinking about the future of sports in this country. The industry has a lot of problems. For example, we lack good management and sponsorship. It is why I am currently building a sports academy on my one-acre piece of land in Kawempe. I will be training interested men and women in different sports, including kickboxing.
When will this academy be ready?
Golola Talent Academy will be ready in two weeks' time since the punching bags have already been put there. It will be a training ground for all kinds of sports. My dream is to increase the number of gold medals coming into Uganda.
Does kickboxing actually pay the bills?
Yes, it does. It is how I have gotten money. When you do kickboxing at a professional level, the money comes in. But I also make money from my work as brand ambassador for different companies.
Is there any other sport you do besides kickboxing?
I do some bit of athletics as well although I am not as fit as Kiprotich. I do body building too but for fun and to encourage those who do it professionally.
Away from work, are you dating?
I love dating but I am not at the moment. I am single, preparing to start searching.
So, what kind of woman are you looking for?
I want a traditional woman. I mean the kind that kneels down to greet me. She should treat me like a king, be educated and in her 30s like me. We will both be mature in the relationship. I do not want women in their 20s because those ones are still young in their thinking; they are good for sugar daddies.
Should she be slim or curvy?
Size, colour and height do not matter. I do not care whether she is fat or small as long as she is a respectful woman. But you know what? I really want a tall woman so that we can have children with American height. She should at least also have big lips so that I can kiss them and make them small.
Talking of children, do you have any?
I have many in that we cannot count them. Someone recently told me that there is a woman in Kalerwe claiming to have my child. I told the person to go and tell her to bring the child so that I can take care of him.
Do you consider yourself a romantic guy?
Yes. Golola is a romantic guy because I have qualities that many women want. I have a good physique and so much energy. I know how to handle women and make love as a grown up man.
What is the one thing about you that most people do not know?
I am a prayer warrior. I talk too much and say so many things but when I get down on my knees to pray, I mean business.
Is Moses Golola actually your real name?
Many times people actually ask me the same question. Let me make it clear today for you and everybody else. Yes, Moses Golola is my real name. I do not want to be like other celebrities who have nicknames because I take pride in my name.
How do you often resolve conflicts?
I walk away from the person and go to pray. People are crazy and provocative. You know the devil is a liar and can challenge you in so many ways even by provoking you through a small man and woman.
Sex versus food
I pick food. Atleast I can go without sex for a year but not food.
Speaking of food, how much do you eat in a day?
It depends on my appetite that day. There are days I will want to take porridge and on other occasions, eat boiled meat, sweet potatoes or even posho and beans. Porridge, however, remains my favourite food and whenever I am taking it, I do not use a cup because it is too small. I take my porridge in a jug regardless of whether it has sugar or not. Any woman interested in me should know how to make porridge.
What do you do for fun?
I love chatting with people and when I am very happy, I dance alone.
Your parting words...
To my fans, thank you for supporting me. I love you so much. Let us fight so much to support each other and not to provoke those who are doing well. If someone does wrong, let us come out and advise them. One word to a wise man is really enough.
Valentine's Day thoughts
What do you think of Valentine's Day?
I think it is a day that should be taken more seriously. It should be a day of renovating love, especially among people who are not happy in their relationships.
If you are never pleased with your husband, not making him breakfast or always facing the wall whenever you are going to sleep, things should change that day. Cook for your husband, make love on that day and get pregnant.
Do you personally celebrate Valentine's Day?
I have never celebrated Valentine's Day because no woman has ever given me a flower and confirmed that they want to enjoy the day with me. It is the reason I have decided to have a kickboxing match on this year's Valentine's Day. I will be fighting Titus Tugume.
Where will the fight take place?
It will be at the MTN Arena, Lugogo. It will start at 7pm. Tickets will be sold at the gate at Shs20,000 and Shs50,000 respectively. Tables will go for Shs1m.

Original_Search_Title: Top Things Seychellois Do On the Weekends
Headline: Seychelles: Top Things Seychellois Do On the Weekends
Body_Text:
Victoria — Seychelles is nothing like the big cities, but locals find a way to unwind when the workweek ends and the weekend arrives. Reporter Salifa Magnan shares her favorite Friday-through-Sunday activities.
1. Catch a wave!
This one is rather obvious. The 115 islands of the Seychelles are surrounded by the Indian Ocean and house hundreds of amazing beaches. An islander just has to grab some yummy snacks, find the most appealing stretch of sand and lay around.
2. Hit the club.
Dress to kill, grab a friend or five and head to a nightlife spot. Want to dance the night away? Hit one of the (admittedly few) nightclubs on the island. My favorite? Tequila Boom night club. Drinking is more your fancy? Then a bar will do the trick. (Just be sure to get a taxi afterwards). I like The Boardwalk Bar at Eden Island.
3. Cruise around the island
It's Sunday and there's nothing to do in the house? No problem amigo. Jump in the car and cruise around the island. Don't forget to grab a few friends or relatives and double the fun. Yes, we're driving in a circle, but there are some fantastic high mountain spots, too.
4. Chill with friends
The venue and activity is no big deal. It's the company that counts, right?
5. Family visits
After one busy week of hard work in the office, the weekend is the only time many families on the island have to see each other. A little gossip thrown around plus a drink or two (mind you, lemonade for the kids) equals a stress-free day.
6. Big lunch
The weekend is a mini-holiday. Families stray away from the average weekday fare and prepare a small feast for the family and friends to enjoy. Our family's favorite weekend dishes? Garlic prawns, sweet and sour chicken and potato and egg salad.Yummy!!!
7. Chores
A bummer, true, but we can't really escape this one for seven straight days? The 'S' days are ideal to get the house squeaky clean (and hope it stays that way).

Original_Search_Title: #RestaurantReview - Nairobi's 360 Degrees Pizza Does Brunch
Headline: Kenya: #RestaurantReview - Nairobi's 360 Degrees Pizza Does Brunch
Body_Text:
Do you need to be Italian to cook great Italian-inspired food? The answer is no. But it's not a bad idea to apprentice under Enzo Coccia, a master pizzaiolo in Napoli, Italy either - that's what 360 Degrees Pizza's head pizzaiolo, Gilbert Ngigi, 27, did for a month.
Ngigi, who started as a waiter at Java, has worked his way up in the food-service industry and has finally found his passion - pizzas. If his pizzas aren't enough of an indication of his passion and dedication for the craft, then a conversation of his different dough recipes will be.
"I have about eight recipes. Depending on the humidity and the time of year, I use a different one."
Depending on the humidity, the amount of water that's needed in the dough can change. Is the dough rising too fast or slow? What's the moisture content? Is the dough becoming more rubber-bandy? All pertinent questions, but probably for many, will never matter unless you're passionate and dedicated to your discipline like this rising Kenyan pizzaiolo much like the loyal staff at 360 Degrees Pizza, which has virtually almost a zero staff turnover. And it's this dedication and passion that has amassed a devout following for 360 Degrees Pizza's Italian-American offering.
360 Degrees Pizza is located at ABC Place, Waiyaki Way, Westlands, Nairobi, Kenya
Reliably one of the most consistent restaurants in Nairobi, 360 Degrees Pizza has found much success in its Italian-American concept. Now the ABC Place restaurant is looking forward to finding more success in opening a new outlet in 2016 and captivating diners with its latest offering - a brunch menu.
Brunch Menu
Available only on the weekends from 8am to 3:30pm; the new menu moves through fruit plates, muesli, eggs benedict, calzones, waffles, pastries and cocktails. It's not really a boring entrée/main/dessert affair so much as a casual place where diners can get a little or a lot of what they fancy - from eggs of any style to Chef's frittata of the day, poached and baked.
The current one-page menu, which will most likely expand, on paper was mouth watering and it was aside from the House-made Chicken and Apple Sausage (KSh300) that arrived dry and weak on seasoning. The wow moment came when the wood-fired Breakfast Calzone (KSh900) filled with roasted peppers, scrambled eggs, arugula, cheddar and ricotta arrived perfectly puffed, pregnant and golden on top. The poached eggs simply oozed beautifully over the English Muffin with Potato Hash, and the Smoked Salmon of the classic Eggs Benedict (KSh1,200) was not unbearably salty. Things got even better when the Caramelized Banana Crepes (KSh550) were livened with Nutella and Whipped Cream, which arrived as a delicate trio of utterly soft and foldable indulgent sweet packages.
The food is generous, but never overwhelming. The menu covers breakfast and lunch - or brunch if you must - with an Italian-American twist. It's nice to find a restaurant that's doing something a bit different from the usual brunch affair, whilst providing a stylish and casual space with attentive and very prompt service, but not in a way that makes you feel rushed.

Original_Search_Title: Mbabazi - Is This How People Live?
Headline: Uganda: Mbabazi - Is This How People Live?
Body_Text:
On the last lap of his campaign in Busoga on Sunday, Independent presidential candidate Amama Mbabazi road-tested his door-door, meet-the-people strategy - with profound results. His first visit was to Samuel Lukungu's homestead at Bwiita-Nakabale village in Namutumba district.
The 66 year-old father of 12 and grandfather of more than 50 looked a typical peasant in the countryside. Lukungu's homestead has four grass-thatched huts and two separate, iron-roofed brick rooms, barely five feet off the ground.
"Does the government operate here? Do you get government services?" a seemingly surprised Mbabazi asked as Lukungu ushered him into his homestead.
Without hesitation, Lukungu opened up to the man who, until September 2013, was prime minister of Uganda.
"We seem to live in a different part of the world," he said. "We have the worst health services, we have no access to safe water; we were left to draw the water for domestic consumption from ponds and a river where other animals drink from."
As they sat down on locally-made stools to talk more, Mbabazi asked his host whether he knew who he was. Lukungu could not put a name to the bespectacled man's face.
"I am Amama Mbabazi, a presidential candidate who is seeking your mandate so that I can change your standard of living," Mbabazi told his host who retorted: "Eeh! You are the one! The name suggested to me that you are a woman, but yes, this is the face I have seen on the [campaign] posters."
Mbabazi's presence in Lukungu's home attracted a small crowd of curious onlookers, among them an elderly woman, Beatrice Babirye, who urged Mbabazi to visit her house too.
"You are at least better than those who fly above us in [a helicopter], they never get a feel of the kind of life we are living in," said Babirye, who looks much older than her age of 67.
She could not believe that she was the same age as the visitor now seeking her vote.
"You look much younger," she said, "But anyway, you live a better life than mine, maybe that is why I look older," said Babirye, a mother of two.
When Mbabazi asked about her daughters, Babirye said they are married, but they are too poor to support her either. Mbabazi then walked to Babirye's house, few metres behind Lukungu's.
As they walked, Babirye insisted that Mbabazi had to see her bedroom. She literary has no beddings save for a worn out mattress. On seeing this, Mbabazi seemed a little lost in his own thoughts for some time.
"Is this how people live?" Mbabazi asked.
TOUCHED
Outside Babirye's two-roomed house was a drunken man who instantly turned into a promoter of Mbabazi's candidature.
"This is why I tell you to vote out Museveni; he is out of touch with the reality," the drunken man said.
Mbabazi later told journalists: "You don't need to ask what their conditions of living are because it is clear. This is a typical example of the abject poverty affecting many of our people across the country.
"These are things that shouldn't be in existence in today's Uganda. They are things that can be sorted out [things like] access to the basics of life such as clean water and basic health care."
In Mbabazi's assessment, Busoga is next to Karamoja in the poverty ratings. In Karamoja, Mbabazi said he wanted to visit at least one homestead there but was failed by state functionaries.
Fighting poverty, he said, is something government can only do through practical interventions.
"Other countries have done it and therefore we also can because what the people want is the encouragement and giving them the means to be productive because they have the energy," Mbabazi said.
'NRM JIGGERS'
Like in other districts Mbabazi has visited, police deployed at all health facilities in the district. To get round the police roadblocks Mbabazi changed tack and visited homesteads.
Mbabazi took everyone by surprise as his convoy suddenly stopped and he walked into Lukungu's homestead. The Observer learnt later that the visit was secretly coordinated by his secretariat staff who worked with one or two local agents to identify the homestead for his visit.
It appeared to have paid off, going by the chants of people in the small crowd outside Lukungu's compound.
"NRM is killing us with jiggers, it is time we get a change in government," shouted one man.
At his main rally in Namutumba town council, Baita Munobwa, a former Busoga kingdom minister, took to the podium and urged people to vote out leaders that
"have kept Busoga in abject poverty."
"I have heard that you grow maize, sweet potatoes and ground nuts. But are you aware that there's a big animal that is eating us up and keeping us in poverty?" Munobwa asked a cheering crowd.
He said the animal is called PIG (poverty, ignorance and greed). Munobwa said poverty and ignorance are among the grassroots people while government is the greedy one feeding off her impoverished people.
NRM FOUNDER
In many places, Mbabazi has been taunted by people donning President Museveni's yellow T-shirts and a sight of them at his rallies in Namutumba brought back memories of the Ntungamo clashes.
Far from the Ntungamo debacle, the people in Museveni's T-shirts at Mbabazi's rallies in Namutumba claimed they were former NRM supporters who had 'gone forward'.
One of them, 38-year-old Robert Mubundu moved atop a truck that went around calling people to the rallies.
"Putting on this T-shirt doesn't in any way imply that I am a Museveni supporter. The T-shirts were distributed free of charge throughout the sub-county [Ivukula] and I couldn't reject it," Mubundu told The Observer on Sunday.
He attributed his change of loyalty to NRM's failed promises. Rachael Waku, 32, a housewife at Ivukula township, wants to see another president.
"I see myself as a Museveni child having been born almost at the time he took power, I have not had an opportunity to compare different regimes and it is for that very reason why I want a change in government," Waku told The Observer.
At his rallies, Mbabazi spoke about his past association with the current NRM leadership that he accused of abandoning the original ideals of the NRM struggle.
"What they are doing is clearly not what took us to the bush; instead, the points I am talking about in this campaign are the original principles of the NRM," Mbabazi said.
He said none of the current NRM leaders can push him out of the party.

Original_Search_Title: Woman in Lilongwe Arrested for Injuring 8 Year Old Girl
Headline: Malawi: Woman in Lilongwe Arrested for Injuring 8 Year Old Girl
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — A 29-year-old woman is in Police custody at Kanengo police station in Lilongwe for allegedly injuring a standard three pupil of Good Hope Primary School in Area 25 on Tuesday.
The woman, Memory Banda who stays close to the school is said to have stoned the girl on her forehead for trespassing on her potato garden as she was coming back from school.
Deputy Head teacher of the school, Yamikani Nyambo confirmed the incident saying it happened around noon when school children used Banda's sweet potato garden as a short cut.
"Pupils who witnessed the incident said the eight-year-old fell unconscious after being hit by stones from Banda on the forehead as she tried to pass through the garden to get to the other side of the road," said Nyambo.
Banda told Mana in an interview that she threw stones at the pupils with the intentions of scaring them. She said she did not mean to hurt the girl who is now recovering at home.
"I have been restricting pupils from passing through the garden for seven days now but they seem not to understand. This caused me marital problems. I tried scaring them away with stones but I did not mean to injure any of them," said Banda who comes from Ntanila village T/A Kaomba, Kasungu district.
Kanengo police spokesperson Esther Mkwanda in a separate interview with Malawi News Agency (Mana) confirmed arresting Banda for injuring the girl who sustained a deep cut on her forehead.
"We have charged Banda with unlawful wounding which is contrary to section 241 of the penal code and it attracts a maximum penalty of seven years imprisonment if found guilty. She will be taken to court on Thursday," said Mkwanda.

Original_Search_Title: Zambia's Ruling Party All Set for Polls With Priest As Top Official
Headline: Zambia's Ruling Party All Set for Polls With Priest As Top Official
Body_Text:
Zambia a country which has enjoyed relatively smooth transitions of power since gaining power from Britain in 1964, will be voting again in a presidential election this year. The poll is set for August 11.
This will be 17 months after incumbent president Edgar Lungu, 59, was elected to replace the late Michael Sata.
Mr Sata, who died in a London Hospital, was the founder of the PF and brought Mr Lungu into the fold.
As part of its preparations for the election, the governing Patriotic Front (PF) has appointed a controversial Catholic priest Fr Frank Bwalya as its spokesman.
ACTIVISM
Father Bwalya, 47, has formed an NGO called Change Life Zambia which he has used as a political tool for his activism.
"I'm still a Catholic priest but on sabbatical leave," he says when asked why he was so deeply involved into politics.
He backed the late President Sata and his group.
He would be found at rallies addressed by Mr Sata where he would offer prayers and counsel the poor over the "sufferings" they were enduring, urging them to vote out the then ruling Movement for Multiparty Democracy (MMD).
During the same period he grew a long beard as part of the protest against the MMD. He was certainly no darling of the regime in power at the time.
When members of the organisation he worked with thought he had crossed the line in his activism, he was disowned.
He walked out on them to form another outfit.
Before he became politically active, Fr Bwalya presided over a Catholic-owned radio station, Icengelo, as station manager in the Copperbelt Province.
He then ran a controversial show prompting a showdown with his superiors leading to his removal.
Government apologists described him as a "nefarious" Catholic priest. Still that never slowed him down.
RISE TO POLITICS
After Mr Sata won the election and unseated the MMD from their 20-year stay in power in 2011, Fr Bwalya was appointed to chair the state power utility board.
He however somehow felt that that was a mere "crumb" for the kind of work he did for the party.
He left the position some months later citing lack of freedom to criticise President Sata's "misrule".
He went into a tirade against Sata's regime calling him chumbu mushololwa (a sweet potato that cannot be straightened out).
The comment did not go down well with President Sata and led to the arrest and prosecution of Fr Bwalya for "defaming" the president.
The two erstwhile buddies had officially fallen out and battle-lines were now drawn. The court later acquitted him.
Most times President Sata was not responding to Fr Bwalya's attacks in the media except on one occasion when he told his party members to "ignore that mad priest," without ever mentioning his name.
Before President Sata's death, the relationship had completely broken down.
Fr Bwalya would later form a political party he called Alliance for a Better Zambia, officially detaching himself from the governing party.
His party was however a botched project. It had no structures and only a handful of members.
BACKED INCUMBENT
Its symbol was a pot. After President Sata's death, Fr Bwalya did not contest but instead strongly backed Mr Lungu on the PF ticket.
He was later to disband his party, and joined the PF.
But even as President Lungu was being accused by party stalwarts of isolating the original members, he moved to appoint Fr Bwalya to the party's supreme decision-making body, the Central Committee.
Days after that announcement was made, Fr Bwalya was again elevated to be chairperson of the party's information committee, effectively making him the party's chief spokesman.
The Catholic Church has yet to comment on these developments.

Original_Search_Title: Region Exchange Seeks Deal With Kigali to Boost Liquidity
Headline: East Africa: Region Exchange Seeks Deal With Kigali to Boost Liquidity
Body_Text:
The East Africa Commodities Exchange is in talks with the Rwanda government to purchase strategic grain reserves using the exchange's electronic platform.
The deal is expected to boost liquidity in the market that is depressed due to low grain production in the region.
"We look to the government to use the exchange as it is a more transparent model for procurement," said Joshua Rugema, the country director at the East Africa Exchange (EAX) Rwanda, after meeting government officials.
Should Rwanda turn to EAX to buy and sell beans and maize to stock its strategic national food reserves, it is hoped other East African Community states will follow suit.
Analysts pushing for governments to list their bids on the commodity exchange board say that besides tapping into bigger and cheaper grain markets, governments will also enjoy a high level of transparency which would check irregular deals. Currently, governments in the region purchase grains through an open tender system that is prone to manipulation.
Boosting volumes
"The move is plausible as biding through the exchange brings in institutional buyers needed to boost volumes. This will also expedite the regional integration of the commodity market," said the executive director of the Rwanda Capital Markets Authority, Robert Mathu.
While EAX is a regional commodity exchange with operations in Kenya and a presence in Uganda through a warehouse operator in Kasese, its market is concentrated in Rwanda. This has limited the volumes traded.
In the 2016 trading season, the market failed to meet the target of 12,000 tonnes of grain it had planned to stock, blaming the shortfall on low supplies. Data from EAX shows that only 6,000 tonnes of maize and beans was handled, eating into commissions for stockbrokers and fees charged by the market management.
According to Mr Mathu, the fact that only a few commodities are traded -- maize beans, soya, sorghum -- contributes to the low volumes of trade on the EAX.
"I appreciate the market is still young but other varieties of commodities are needed on the market," he said.
The exchange is looking to Kenya, the largest grain producer in the region, to boost the volumes traded on its platform and handled in its warehouse. It has signed big trading contracts with Unga, Jamii and Mombasa Maize Millers in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Why? My People, Why? What Has Reduced us to Savagery?
Headline: Uganda: Why? My People, Why? What Has Reduced us to Savagery?
Body_Text:
Dear Tingasiga:
A Facebook friend, posted a video recording last week of a very dangerous and chaotic scene where people wearing yellow tops, presumably Kampala Capital City Authority (KCCA) officers, together with other armed people in police uniforms, and others in plain clothes, very roughly manhandle a woman with a baby strapped to her body.
The captors drag the petite woman and her baby (we presume) on a concrete surface, towards a pick-up truck. At the same time, a man in a white and black checked shirt grabs a screaming little girl, lifts her by the arm like a chicken or small animal, and follows in the direction of the chaos.
The woman and baby are dragged past a black/blue police vehicle. A policewoman, clutching a long semi-automatic weapon, assists with the macabre process. Five other police officers, some with helmets, are present. They clear the path but make no obvious contact with the captives.
Mother and baby, screaming and flailing, are lifted off the pavement and thrown onto the cargo bed like a sack of sorghum. There is an audible thud as the mother/baby couplet land.
Two women, with red identity numbers, 167 and 333 respectively, printed on their yellow T-shirts, are among the people who have just thrown this mother and child onto the truck.
The woman promptly gets up, perhaps in an attempt to jump off the truck. Her protective maternal reflexes undiminished, she senses her baby's head dangerously close to the side of the cargo bed. She places her left hand on the baby's head as she scrambles to her feet.
The baby, now completely upside down, still strapped to the mother's side, is crying and thrusting her legs in the air. It is only by God's grace that the baby does not drop headfirst onto the hard-metal floor of the cargo bed.
The woman with ID No. 333 pushes the mother back. Hands appear from the other side of the truck and grab the woman's torn skirt, briefly exposing her undergarment. Her blouse has been pulled up, exposing her back.
A man in plainclothes climbs onto the truck, spreads his legs and stands over the woman and her baby. His left leg traps the baby against the mother as he turns her to the right, pushes her down and then sits on the ledge of the truck. He pulls the woman's arms above her head, paying no attention to the dangling baby.
Amid the woman's cries of "nta!" (let go), the man with the checked shirt throws the little girl onto the cargo bed. She remains standing for a few seconds, very frightened and crying, before a man's hands push her down to the floor. The man jumps onto the truck and grabs the toddler's right arm.
Meanwhile, the man restraining the woman applies very firm finger and thumb pressure to the back of her neck ("The Death Touch"), probably aiming to knock her out by compressing her carotid arteries (which take blood to the brain.)
The trio, now somewhat subdued, are driven off, presumably under arrest for some unknown infraction. The entire thing is over in less than two minutes.
Throughout the whole fracas, there is a large crowd of spectators. Most are very concerned about the children's safety, but the captors ignore their appeals for gentleness.
My immediate reaction after watching the episode was: Why? My people, why? What has happened to us, reduced to savagery in the name of enforcing "the law?" Why are these things happening under the watch of two women, Beti Namisango Kamya, the Minister for Kampala, and Jennifer Musisi, executive director of KCCA? The woman's "crime" and the state of her mental health are immaterial to the manner of her arrest. It is extreme cruelty to the woman, to the children, to the spectators and to humanity.
It reveals a reckless disregard for the dignity of other people's lives. This woman and her children are treated like non-humans. They are denied rights that their captors and the captors' bosses expect for themselves and their children.
The violent arrest endangers the lives of all three, with real risks of physical injury and even death to the children.
An almost certain consequence is post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) which may affect all, including the little baby.
There is extensive paediatric and child psychiatry literature that affirms the common sense knowledge that the violence that children experience or witness has intense consequences on their health and development. It is very likely to cause physical, behavioural and lasting psychological health problems.
I appeal to Kamya and Musisi to place human rights and dignity for all above everything they do. It does not matter what a person's social, economic or mental health status is. We are all children of God, with human rights enshrined in the Uganda Constitution, the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child. These, of course, were already wonderfully summarised by Jesus Christ, as reported in Luke 6:31 - "Do to others as you would have them do to you."

Original_Search_Title: Headlines of Newspapers Issued in Khartoum On Sunday October 16, 2016
Headline: Sudan: Headlines of Newspapers Issued in Khartoum On Sunday October 16, 2016
Body_Text:
